item.title: The 16 Perfectly Reachable Habits That Made Me Who I Am
item.dc:creator: Billy Oppenhiemer
item.description: For 2,130 consecutive games, Lou Gehrig played first base for the New York Yankees‚Äîa streak that would stand for five and a half decades. He put up a career .340 batting average. He was a two-time MVP, seven-time All-Star, six-time World Series Champion, Hall of Famer, and the first player ever to have his number retired. All from a man who wasn‚Äôt born an athlete. As a boy, Lou Gehrig was so uncoordinated that a friend joked his body ‚Äúbehaved as if it were drunk.‚Äù He made himself into the Iron Horse‚Äîday by day, action by action‚Äînot with some special gift, but with a handful of perfectly reachable habits. He went to bed early every night. He didn‚Äôt drink or smoke. He showed up even when he didn‚Äôt feel like it. He never made excuses. He trained on his own. He saved his money. The only thing he ever asked of the staff was a stick of gum on his locker before a game. Two if it was a doubleheader. Nothing particularly groundbreaking or dramatic. But strung together, day after day, year after year, those small things added up to one of the greatest careers in the history of the sport. And that‚Äôs what the path to greatness almost always looks like: small, ordinary actions, repeated for a very long time. That‚Äôs how I‚Äôve tried to build my own life. (It‚Äôs also what we built the Habits for Success, Habits for Happiness course around at Daily Stoic. It‚Äôs an awesome six-week experience‚Äîinspired by the best habits of Marcus Aurelius, Seneca, and Epictetus, as well as my own‚Äîthat thousands of people have now gone through).¬Ý And so that‚Äôs what I wanted to share today‚Äîthe handful of ordinary habits and practices that, repeated again and again, have had an enormous impact. On my work. On my health. On my happiness. And most of all, on who I am. ‚Äì Wake up early. I get up early, between 5:30 or 6am.The mornings are the most productive hours of the day‚Äîbefore the interruptions, before the distractions, before the rest of the world gets up and going too. Toni Morrison found she was just more confident in the morning, before the day had exacted its toll and the mind was fresh. Like most of us, she realized she was just ‚Äúnot very bright or very witty or very inventive after the sun goes down.‚Äù Who can be? After a day of banal conversations, frustrations, mistakes, and exhaustion. As I said in Discipline is Destiny, this decision we make in the morning, it not only determines how our day will go but it determines who we are. ‚Äì Protect the best part of the day. The novelist Philipp Meyer (whose book ‚Äã‚ÄãThe Son‚Äã‚Äã is an incredible read) ‚Äã‚Äãtold me on the podcast‚Äã‚Äã, ‚ÄúYou have to be very careful about to what (and to whom) you‚Äôre giving the best part of your day.‚Äù My assistant knows not to schedule anything before mid-morning because early calls and meetings don‚Äôt just take time‚Äîthey sap the energy needed for the essential work. I want to give my best self to my most important things. Everything else can come after.¬Ý ‚Äì No phone for the first hour. My rule is no phone for at least the first hour of the day. Some days, I don‚Äôt touch it until lunch, and on those days I‚Äôm happier and more productive. ‚Äì Journal. I‚Äôve been keeping a One Line a Day journal for nine years now (you should keep one, they‚Äôre amazing). One line. One sentence. What did I think about today? Where did I go? What happened? That‚Äôs it. It sounds like nothing‚Äîand in a way it is‚Äîbut the words accumulate. It’s weird how much you don’t remember, even of your own life. Most of what‚Äôs in there I’d completely forgotten. Without those pages, it would all be gone. With them, I can flip back and I see the person I was. I can see the person I am becoming. I can see the person I want to be again. I can see mistakes I don‚Äôt want to make anymore. I can see all the small things that have added up to who I am.¬Ý ‚Äì Just a little progress every day. As I wrote about recently, my writing habit was all-or-nothing for a long time‚Äîeither I wrote a lot of words or I didn‚Äôt. Over time, I‚Äôve lowered the stakes: now the question is simply, ‚ÄúDid I make a positive contribution to my writing today?‚Äù Sometimes that means writing, sometimes editing, adding, deleting. Sometimes I‚Äôm home and it‚Äôs in my office, sometimes I‚Äôm on the road and it‚Äôs on a plane or in a hotel room. Sometimes it‚Äôs a big contribution, sometimes it‚Äôs a little contribution.¬Ý ‚Äì Carry a book everywhere. People often assume I‚Äôm a speed reader. It‚Äôs the most common email I get. They see all the books I recommend every month in my reading newsletter and assume I must have some secret, some trick for reading so fast. The truth is, even though I read hundreds of books each year, I read at a pretty slow pace. In fact, I deliberately read slowly. But I also read all the time. I always carry a book with me. Phone, wallet, keys‚Äîand a book. I‚Äôve brought one to the Grammys, NFL games, kids‚Äô practices, restaurants, the DMV, Disneyland, the White House, backstage before talks, and into the waiting room before surgery. Every time I get a second, I crack it open. ‚Äì Take a walk. I take a walk twice a day. I don‚Äôt think I have ever taken a walk without thinking, after, ‚ÄúI am so glad I did that.‚Äù We rescued a dog last year and that‚Äôs been the best part. It‚Äôs ‚Äúforced‚Äù me to do something I love to do. It‚Äôs not about burning calories, but for the philosophical and psychological benefits. As Nietzsche would later say: ‚ÄúIt is only ideas gained from walking that have any [...]
item.content:encoded:
For 2,130 consecutive games, Lou Gehrig played first base for the New York Yankees‚Äîa streak that would stand for five and a half decades. He put up a career .340 batting average. He was a two-time MVP, seven-time All-Star, six-time World Series Champion, Hall of Famer, and the first player ever to have his number retired.

All from a man who wasn‚Äôt born an athlete. As a boy, Lou Gehrig was so uncoordinated that a friend joked his body ‚Äúbehaved as if it were drunk.‚Äù He made himself into the Iron Horse‚Äîday by day, action by action‚Äînot with some special gift, but with a handful of perfectly reachable habits. He went to bed early every night. He didn‚Äôt drink or smoke. He showed up even when he didn‚Äôt feel like it. He never made excuses. He trained on his own. He saved his money. The only thing he ever asked of the staff was a stick of gum on his locker before a game. Two if it was a doubleheader.

Nothing particularly groundbreaking or dramatic. But strung together, day after day, year after year, those small things added up to one of the greatest careers in the history of the sport. And that‚Äôs what the path to greatness almost always looks like: small, ordinary actions, repeated for a very long time.

That‚Äôs how I‚Äôve tried to build my own life. (It‚Äôs also what we built the Habits for Success, Habits for Happiness course around at Daily Stoic. It‚Äôs an awesome six-week experience‚Äîinspired by the best habits of Marcus Aurelius, Seneca, and Epictetus, as well as my own‚Äîthat thousands of people have now gone through).¬Ý

And so that‚Äôs what I wanted to share today‚Äîthe handful of ordinary habits and practices that, repeated again and again, have had an enormous impact. On my work. On my health. On my happiness. And most of all, on who I am.

‚Äì Wake up early. I get up early, between 5:30 or 6am.The mornings are the most productive hours of the day‚Äîbefore the interruptions, before the distractions, before the rest of the world gets up and going too. Toni Morrison found she was just more confident in the morning, before the day had exacted its toll and the mind was fresh. Like most of us, she realized she was just ‚Äúnot very bright or very witty or very inventive after the sun goes down.‚Äù Who can be? After a day of banal conversations, frustrations, mistakes, and exhaustion. As I said in Discipline is Destiny, this decision we make in the morning, it not only determines how our day will go but it determines who we are.

‚Äì Protect the best part of the day. The novelist Philipp Meyer (whose book ‚Äã‚ÄãThe Son‚Äã‚Äã is an incredible read) ‚Äã‚Äãtold me on the podcast‚Äã‚Äã, ‚ÄúYou have to be very careful about to what (and to whom) you‚Äôre giving the best part of your day.‚Äù My assistant knows not to schedule anything before mid-morning because early calls and meetings don‚Äôt just take time‚Äîthey sap the energy needed for the essential work. I want to give my best self to my most important things. Everything else can come after.¬Ý

‚Äì No phone for the first hour. My rule is no phone for at least the first hour of the day. Some days, I don‚Äôt touch it until lunch, and on those days I‚Äôm happier and more productive.

‚Äì Journal. I‚Äôve been keeping a One Line a Day journal for nine years now (you should keep one, they‚Äôre amazing). One line. One sentence. What did I think about today? Where did I go? What happened? That‚Äôs it. It sounds like nothing‚Äîand in a way it is‚Äîbut the words accumulate. It’s weird how much you don’t remember, even of your own life. Most of what‚Äôs in there I’d completely forgotten. Without those pages, it would all be gone. With them, I can flip back and I see the person I was. I can see the person I am becoming. I can see the person I want to be again. I can see mistakes I don‚Äôt want to make anymore. I can see all the small things that have added up to who I am.¬Ý

‚Äì Just a little progress every day. As I wrote about recently, my writing habit was all-or-nothing for a long time‚Äîeither I wrote a lot of words or I didn‚Äôt. Over time, I‚Äôve lowered the stakes: now the question is simply, ‚ÄúDid I make a positive contribution to my writing today?‚Äù Sometimes that means writing, sometimes editing, adding, deleting. Sometimes I‚Äôm home and it‚Äôs in my office, sometimes I‚Äôm on the road and it‚Äôs on a plane or in a hotel room. Sometimes it‚Äôs a big contribution, sometimes it‚Äôs a little contribution.¬Ý

‚Äì Carry a book everywhere. People often assume I‚Äôm a speed reader. It‚Äôs the most common email I get. They see all the books I recommend every month in my reading newsletter and assume I must have some secret, some trick for reading so fast. The truth is, even though I read hundreds of books each year, I read at a pretty slow pace. In fact, I deliberately read slowly. But I also read all the time. I always carry a book with me. Phone, wallet, keys‚Äîand a book. I‚Äôve brought one to the Grammys, NFL games, kids‚Äô practices, restaurants, the DMV, Disneyland, the White House, backstage before talks, and into the waiting room before surgery. Every time I get a second, I crack it open.

‚Äì Take a walk. I take a walk twice a day. I don‚Äôt think I have ever taken a walk without thinking, after, ‚ÄúI am so glad I did that.‚Äù We rescued a dog last year and that‚Äôs been the best part. It‚Äôs ‚Äúforced‚Äù me to do something I love to do. It‚Äôs not about burning calories, but for the philosophical and psychological benefits. As Nietzsche would later say: ‚ÄúIt is only ideas gained from walking that have any worth.‚Äù¬Ý

‚Äì Strenuous exercise. I also go for a run or swim just about every day. ‚ÄúWe treat the body rigorously,‚Äù Seneca said, ‚Äúso that it‚Äôs not disobedient to the mind.‚Äù That sounds a little aggressive, because in my experience, the physical practice is actually quite kind to the mind. Some days, it turns it off in a very restorative way. Other days, it lets it wander and work on things. I can‚Äôt tell you how many times I‚Äôve thought of a great line or solved an intractable writing problem while running or swimming. Exercise is also an easy win every day. Writing can go poorly, but going on a run or swim always goes well.¬Ý

‚Äì A simple to-do list. When we were working on ‚Äã‚Äã‚ÄãWhat You‚Äôre Made For‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã, the late George Raveling told me that when he became Nike‚Äôs Director of International Basketball at 63, having no prior corporate experience, he was overwhelmed by self-doubt. Until a mentor gave him a simple system: ‚ÄúWhen you leave the office every day, leave a yellow pad in the middle of the desk, and when you come in the morning, write down the three most important things you gotta get done that day in that order. That day, do not do anything else but the first thing on the pad. And if you get the first one, then you go to the second one.‚Äù As for me‚Äîevery day, on a notecard, I write down 5-6 things I want to get done that day. Every day, I cross these off and tear up the card. That‚Äôs it. That‚Äôs the system.¬Ý

‚Äì Don‚Äôt drink. ‚ÄúWhy don‚Äôt you drink?‚Äù is a question I’ve been asked probably eight thousand times in my life. The real answer is boring: I don’t like the taste. But even if I did, I still wouldn’t. Heraclitus‚Äôs line was that ‚Äúa dry soul is wisest and best.‚Äù He‚Äôs right. Have you ever done anything you‚Äôre really proud of while drinking? Is anyone their best selves while drunk? Of course not.

‚Äì Don‚Äôt drink calories. This is one I got from Tim Ferriss. Along with not drinking alcohol, I don‚Äôt drink soda, smoothies, juice, milk, anything. It‚Äôs just a simple way to help keep your diet on track.

‚Äì Focus on process, not goals. As I wrote about recently, I don‚Äôt have goals. When I write, I don‚Äôt focus on finishing books‚Äîthat would be overwhelming. Instead, I focus on my notecard system and writing for a couple hours every day. The books emerge from that process naturally, over time.

‚Äì Think about death. Shakespeare said that every third thought should be of our grave. Perhaps that‚Äôs too much. One thought per day is plenty. I keep a coin in my pocket to remind me of this and touch it at least once a day. Death doesn‚Äôt make life pointless but rather purposeful. And fortunately, we don‚Äôt have to nearly die to tap into this.

‚Äì Review the day. ‚ÄúWhen the light has been removed and my wife has fallen silent,‚Äù Seneca wrote, ‚ÄúI examine my entire day and go back over what I‚Äôve done and said, hiding nothing from myself, passing nothing by.‚Äù He’d ask himself: What bad habits did I cure? What temptations did I resist? In what specific way am I now better than I was yesterday? I spend some time every night on my own version. Did I follow my plans for the day? Was I prepared enough? What could I do better? What have I learned that will help me tomorrow? These simple questions make an enormous impact. So I spend some time every night answering them.

‚Äì Sleep with the phone in another room. Ben Sasse used to give his young staffers old-school alarm clocks‚Äînot because he wanted to make sure they were on time for work, but so they wouldn‚Äôt have an excuse to sleep with their phone on the nightstand. If your alarm isn‚Äôt your phone, your phone can go in the other room. And if your phone is in the other room, you can‚Äôt check it at night. Which means I won‚Äôt know if a text or an email comes in. I won‚Äôt be tempted to scroll. I’ll have to lie there with my own thoughts, read a book, or maybe even go to sleep at a reasonable time.

‚Äì Go the F*ck to Sleep! I follow the advice of a book I love to read to my kids: Go the F*ck to Sleep! All the other habits and practices listed here become irrelevant if I don‚Äôt have the energy and clarity to do them. In the military, they speak of sleep discipline‚Äîmeaning it‚Äôs something you have to be good at, you have to be conscious of, something you can‚Äôt let slip. We only have so much energy for our work, for our relationships, for ourselves. Knowing this, I guard my sleep carefully. I try to get my 7-8 hours of sleep every night.

item.title: This Is Always Worth All The Trouble (Or So I Tell Myself)
item.dc:creator: Billy Oppenhiemer
item.description: There are a million good reasons not to travel. I woke up with a bunch of them this morning.¬Ý I was exhausted. My kids were fighting. My wife has been sick. It was ungodly hot‚Äîliterally the hottest Europe has ever been. My routine is out the window. So is any sense of a budget. Our suitcases are already full of knick-nacks and crap we don‚Äôt need and there‚Äôs quite a bit of trip left. The car that was supposed to pick us up for our tour is late.¬Ý But we‚Äôre in Rome.¬Ý It‚Äôs all worth it‚Ä¶or so I tell myself. Even though travel can be expensive, stressful, and frustrating. Even though it tests your patience. Even though things go wrong. Even though you know that when you get home, you‚Äôll be behind on everything. Still, it‚Äôs worth it.¬Ý It has always been worth it.¬Ý¬Ý For all their warnings against escapism, the Stoics traveled widely‚Äîin an age when travel was far slower, harder, and more dangerous.¬Ý Unlike his stepfather Antoninus, who never left Italy, Marcus Aurelius was on the road a lot as emperor. It wasn‚Äôt always pleasant. But it undoubtedly influenced his philosophy and his view of the world. He could have sent someone else to inspect the troops on his behalf, but he chose to go. When the calls of duty and the road converged, he went. Seneca was born in Corduba, Spain (modern C√≥rdoba), moved to Rome, and in addition to his exile in Corsica, lived for nearly a decade in Egypt. Posidonius (a key figure in Middle Stoicism) worked and lived in many places throughout his long life‚ÄîSyria, Athens, Rome, and Rhodes‚Äîand traveled almost the entirety of the known world. After completing his studies under Posidonius, Athenodorus traveled widely as a lecturer, reaching Petra, Egypt, and other major Mediterranean cities before becoming young Octavian‚Äôs teacher in Apollonia, on the coast of modern Albania. These were critical experiences for them. Then, and now, hitting the road was a source of wisdom. It shaped their philosophy. It enlarged their view of the world. It opened their eyes to new lands, new customs, new perspectives. ‚ÄúTravel is fatal to prejudice, bigotry, and narrow-mindedness,‚Äù Mark Twain famously wrote, ‚Äúand many of our people need it sorely on these accounts.‚Äù¬Ý This was true of America in the 19th and 20th centuries, and it was true in Rome in the first century. Marcus Aurelius‚Äô passages in Meditations which reference being a citizen of the world were almost certainly shaped by the people he met in Athens and Tarsus and Alexandria (and of course the people he met in Rome who had traveled there from far away).¬Ý Every culture thinks its norms are, well, normal. One of my favorite examples of this is the criticism I get that by talking about politics I am ‚Äúupsetting half my audience.‚Äù It‚Äôs the most American thing in the world to assume that the world is as evenly divided as America is. (Shocker, it‚Äôs not!)¬Ý Only travel can shake us from this ethnocentrism, awakening us to the plain fact that our way of life is but one of many. In our travels, we meet people who care about different things, wear different clothes, tell different stories, eat different foods, speak different languages.¬Ý When he traveled, Montaigne sucked up everything he saw like a vacuum, avoiding much in the way of plans, leaving himself open to chance encounters and experiences. Like the time he came across men leading two ostriches on leashes as a present for a duke. He stopped in at every church he could find, taking in their various practices like an anthropologist. When the New World was discovered‚Äîbasically in his lifetime‚ÄîMontaigne did not think the people who lived there were savages. From his travels, he understood that every distant land has its own culture and beliefs‚Äîand that our customs would seem just as barbaric to them. Not that he defended the ‚Äúhorrible barbarity‚Äù of cannibalism. What struck him was that ‚Äúwhile judging correctly of their wrong-doings we should be so blind to our own.‚Äù Did the cannibals draw and quarter each other? Commit atrocities? Accuse each other of black magic? Who was actually the barbarian? Perhaps it was a lesson he first absorbed from his extensive reading of Herodotus, our first great historian and travel writer, who traveled thousands over the course of his life‚Äîfrom Persia to Athens to Italy, Libya, Babylonia, Byzantium, and the Black Sea. Above all, he delighted in getting inside the nomos of each place‚Äîthe people‚Äôs customs, and the ways they differed from his own. He loved to tell the story about a small sect in India that ate a small piece of their dead as part of a religious ritual. Obscene! Disgusting! But how did these people react when told that Greeks burned their dead at the funeral pyre? An outrage! A sacrilege! Me and Herodotus in the Palazzo Massimo The world is very big. The place you call home is only one small corner of it. There is wisdom on the other side of the hill, wisdom in the experiences that only the road can give you, wisdom in learning how other people live and why they live that way. It‚Äôs easy to be comfortable where you are. To tell yourself that travel isn‚Äôt worth all the hassle. To think of it as a luxury, an indulgence, a needless extravagance. But it isn‚Äôt. It‚Äôs essential. ‚ÄúI was never a fan of people who don‚Äôt leave home,‚Äù the well-traveled Joan Didion once wrote. ‚ÄúIt just seems like part of your duty in life.‚Äù But leaving home doesn‚Äôt have to mean going far. It doesn‚Äôt have to mean spending lots of money on some exotic trip to the other side of the world. I remember talking to a waitress in Natchez, Mississippi once who told me she had never been to Vicksburg (a little over an hour away!). Growing up in the suburbs of Sacramento, I was lucky my parents took [...]
item.content:encoded:
There are a million good reasons not to travel.

I woke up with a bunch of them this morning.¬Ý

I was exhausted. My kids were fighting. My wife has been sick. It was ungodly hot‚Äîliterally the hottest Europe has ever been. My routine is out the window. So is any sense of a budget. Our suitcases are already full of knick-nacks and crap we don‚Äôt need and there‚Äôs quite a bit of trip left. The car that was supposed to pick us up for our tour is late.¬Ý

But we‚Äôre in Rome.¬Ý

It‚Äôs all worth it‚Ä¶or so I tell myself.

Even though travel can be expensive, stressful, and frustrating. Even though it tests your patience. Even though things go wrong. Even though you know that when you get home, you‚Äôll be behind on everything.

Still, it‚Äôs worth it.¬Ý

It has always been worth it.¬Ý¬Ý

For all their warnings against escapism, the Stoics traveled widely‚Äîin an age when travel was far slower, harder, and more dangerous.¬Ý

Unlike his stepfather Antoninus, who never left Italy, Marcus Aurelius was on the road a lot as emperor. It wasn‚Äôt always pleasant. But it undoubtedly influenced his philosophy and his view of the world. He could have sent someone else to inspect the troops on his behalf, but he chose to go. When the calls of duty and the road converged, he went. Seneca was born in Corduba, Spain (modern C√≥rdoba), moved to Rome, and in addition to his exile in Corsica, lived for nearly a decade in Egypt. Posidonius (a key figure in Middle Stoicism) worked and lived in many places throughout his long life‚ÄîSyria, Athens, Rome, and Rhodes‚Äîand traveled almost the entirety of the known world. After completing his studies under Posidonius, Athenodorus traveled widely as a lecturer, reaching Petra, Egypt, and other major Mediterranean cities before becoming young Octavian‚Äôs teacher in Apollonia, on the coast of modern Albania.

These were critical experiences for them. Then, and now, hitting the road was a source of wisdom. It shaped their philosophy. It enlarged their view of the world. It opened their eyes to new lands, new customs, new perspectives.

‚ÄúTravel is fatal to prejudice, bigotry, and narrow-mindedness,‚Äù Mark Twain famously wrote, ‚Äúand many of our people need it sorely on these accounts.‚Äù¬Ý

This was true of America in the 19th and 20th centuries, and it was true in Rome in the first century. Marcus Aurelius‚Äô passages in Meditations which reference being a citizen of the world were almost certainly shaped by the people he met in Athens and Tarsus and Alexandria (and of course the people he met in Rome who had traveled there from far away).¬Ý

Every culture thinks its norms are, well, normal. One of my favorite examples of this is the criticism I get that by talking about politics I am ‚Äúupsetting half my audience.‚Äù It‚Äôs the most American thing in the world to assume that the world is as evenly divided as America is. (Shocker, it‚Äôs not!)¬Ý

Only travel can shake us from this ethnocentrism, awakening us to the plain fact that our way of life is but one of many. In our travels, we meet people who care about different things, wear different clothes, tell different stories, eat different foods, speak different languages.¬Ý

When he traveled, Montaigne sucked up everything he saw like a vacuum, avoiding much in the way of plans, leaving himself open to chance encounters and experiences. Like the time he came across men leading two ostriches on leashes as a present for a duke. He stopped in at every church he could find, taking in their various practices like an anthropologist.

When the New World was discovered‚Äîbasically in his lifetime‚ÄîMontaigne did not think the people who lived there were savages. From his travels, he understood that every distant land has its own culture and beliefs‚Äîand that our customs would seem just as barbaric to them. Not that he defended the ‚Äúhorrible barbarity‚Äù of cannibalism. What struck him was that ‚Äúwhile judging correctly of their wrong-doings we should be so blind to our own.‚Äù Did the cannibals draw and quarter each other? Commit atrocities? Accuse each other of black magic? Who was actually the barbarian?

Perhaps it was a lesson he first absorbed from his extensive reading of Herodotus, our first great historian and travel writer, who traveled thousands over the course of his life‚Äîfrom Persia to Athens to Italy, Libya, Babylonia, Byzantium, and the Black Sea. Above all, he delighted in getting inside the nomos of each place‚Äîthe people‚Äôs customs, and the ways they differed from his own. He loved to tell the story about a small sect in India that ate a small piece of their dead as part of a religious ritual. Obscene! Disgusting! But how did these people react when told that Greeks burned their dead at the funeral pyre? An outrage! A sacrilege!

Me and Herodotus in the Palazzo Massimo

The world is very big. The place you call home is only one small corner of it. There is wisdom on the other side of the hill, wisdom in the experiences that only the road can give you, wisdom in learning how other people live and why they live that way.

It‚Äôs easy to be comfortable where you are. To tell yourself that travel isn‚Äôt worth all the hassle. To think of it as a luxury, an indulgence, a needless extravagance. But it isn‚Äôt. It‚Äôs essential. ‚ÄúI was never a fan of people who don‚Äôt leave home,‚Äù the well-traveled Joan Didion once wrote. ‚ÄúIt just seems like part of your duty in life.‚Äù

But leaving home doesn‚Äôt have to mean going far. It doesn‚Äôt have to mean spending lots of money on some exotic trip to the other side of the world. I remember talking to a waitress in Natchez, Mississippi once who told me she had never been to Vicksburg (a little over an hour away!). Growing up in the suburbs of Sacramento, I was lucky my parents took us on trips to Europe and China and Canada and Mexico, but I BARELY ever went to downtown Sacramento. Or even San Francisco. I only discovered the fullness of those cities as an adult.¬Ý

Don‚Äôt tell yourself you‚Äôll do it later. When you‚Äôre older. When the kids are older. When things settle down. When you have more money. When it‚Äôs more convenient. That misses the point. You don‚Äôt need the benefits of travel someday, when your life is perfectly arranged for it. You need them now.

So hit the road! Travel to the well-known places and the little seen. Travel by boat and by car and by train and by plane and by foot. Read the signs. Struggle with the language barrier. See things from the other side of the road. Eat the food. Go to the museums. Sit at sidewalk cafes. See the ancient hieroglyphs and notice graffiti on city streets. See the cemeteries. Visit the battlefields. Listen to people chatter. Learn. Explore. Be curious. Ask questions. Carry a notebook. Write down everything you see and feel and think.

See your own country as a tourist. Live abroad like a local. Every building, every person has a story. Think of what they witnessed. Think of who stood where you are standing. Think of what was here before any of this was here. Think of what people used to think here, what they were afraid of, what they hoped for, what they believed.

Let things go wrong (and they will!). These experiences teach as much as anything else. Last summer, I spent 27 days in Greece with my family, covering some 3,000 kilometers between Athens, Olympia, Ithaca, Delphi, Thermopylae, Mount Olympus, Marathon, and Cape Sounio. We were in a Chinese rental car that was far too big for tiny ancient streets. We got a flat tire at the top of Mount Olympus. I was stung by a jellyfish near Thermopylae. We bumped into friends I hadn‚Äôt seen since high school. We got ripped off. On the last day, a bee stung me in the back of the throat and I almost died. Oh, and somewhere in between there, I got myself a bad case of sunstroke.¬Ý

But that‚Äôs what travel is supposed to be. Travel is an adventure. Travel is pushing your limits. Travel is trying new things. Travel is learning that most problems can be solved, most setbacks can be survived, and most of what goes wrong can be figured out.¬Ý

You don‚Äôt have to be gone for long. You don‚Äôt have to spend a fortune. You don‚Äôt have to see the whole world to become a worldly person.

But you have to check out what‚Äôs on the other side of the hill a couple times.

You have to expose yourself to different places, different people, different perspectives.

You have to hit the road.

It will be disruptive. Your patience will be tested. Things will go wrong.

And it will all be worth it.

item.title: 39 (Or So) Lessons On The Way To 39
item.dc:creator: Ryan Holiday
item.description: Well, I‚Äôm now almost 40. Like officially almost 40. I woke up yesterday in Cle Elum, Washington, ran 20 miles and then turned 39. I did a little talk for Amazon and then flew home, getting in very late and very tired.¬Ý I have been doing some version of this post for basically twenty years.¬Ý (You can see my birthday posts for ‚Äã26‚Äã, ‚Äã27‚Äã, ‚Äã28‚Äã, ‚Äã29‚Äã, ‚Äã30‚Äã, ‚Äã31‚Äã, ‚Äã32, ‚Äã‚Äã33‚Äã, ‚Äã34‚Äã, ‚Äã35‚Äã, ‚Äã36‚Äã, 37, and 38‚Äã).¬Ý How much have I learned and changed? Quite a bit, I‚Äôm proud to say. Not enough, I‚Äôd have to add much more honestly. I still fall prey to the same patterns. I am still doing too many of the same things. And worse, I‚Äôm still too often not aware of it until it‚Äôs painfully obvious much later. Which brings me to my first lesson for this year‚Äôs list.¬Ý ‚Äì At my talk in Seattle last week (I‚Äôm on tour this summer and fall if you want to come see me in the US and Australia), someone asked how I would describe myself in one word. I thought about it and I just said: ‚ÄúTrying.‚Äù I‚Äôm trying. Could I try a little harder? Would I like to make a little more progress? Yes. But I am trying.¬Ý ‚Äì I‚Äôve given hundreds of talks now, and I could give the same one every night, the same way, until I had it down cold. Instead, I try to change it each time‚ÄîI add things, I cut things, I work out new material right there on stage. If I have the opportunity, because of the audience or whatever, to give a totally new one that I haven‚Äôt done before, I see that as a gift not an obligation. Because harder is what makes me better. It also keeps life interesting.¬Ý ‚Äì One of the things that sneaks up on you as you get older are the blocks of time you can start measuring things in. For a while, four years‚Äîthe length of high school or college‚Äîis a measuring stick of something that takes a while. Then you get comfortable with things being a decade long‚Äî‚ÄúWe‚Äôve been friends of a decade‚Äù and ‚ÄúThat was like ten years ago?‚Äù‚Äîand then suddenly, it‚Äôs twenty years, like I did in the intro above. I had my twenty year high school reunion last year. This gives you perspective. You start to get a sense of how long things take and how much capacity you have to endure or live through. ‚Äì And yet even so, the passage of time can sneak up on you. I was looking at a baseball picture of my son the other day. It was him holding a bat, looking basically like the kid he is now, except it says 2025. How could he be old enough to at one point have been five years younger? I was trying to express to him how unbelievable that is to me. But it‚Äôs true. Tempus fugit the ancients said. Time flies. Be present. Be ready.¬Ý ‚Äì I don‚Äôt believe in time travel‚Ä¶but I do believe the work‚Äîlearning, research, exercise, etc‚Äîis a gift we give our future selves. Basically, you can‚Äôt make withdrawals from accounts you haven‚Äôt made deposits in.¬Ý¬Ý ‚Äì The purpose of any piece of writing at all is not the end product on the page. It‚Äôs the person YOU are on the other side of having done it. It‚Äôs the thinking long and hard about something. It‚Äôs the slow, tedious, difficult work of figuring out what you actually work. And the equally hard work of finding the words for what you think. AI can give you an essay, an article, a book, or a briefing. What it can‚Äôt give you is the person you can only become by doing the writing yourself. ‚Äì ‚ÄúIt is surprising,‚Äù Roosevelt said, ‚Äúhow much reading a man can do in time usually wasted.‚Äù That‚Äôs exactly right. Bring a book with you everywhere.¬Ý ‚Äì I don‚Äôt think I have ever taken a walk without thinking, after, ‚ÄúI am so glad I did that.‚Äù We rescued a dog last year and that‚Äôs been the best part. It‚Äôs ‚Äúforced‚Äù me to do something I love to do.¬Ý ‚Äì For fifteen years (again those chunks of time!) I‚Äôd had this idea sitting in the back of my head, and last July I finally did it: I ran the 26 miles from Marathon to Athens. On the Daily Stoic podcast, Jesse Itzler told me about this concept of the Misogi. Borrowed from an ancient Japanese purification ritual, the modern Misogi is about committing to one epic, year-defining challenge‚Äîsomething so significant, so hard, so memorable, that decades later, when you think back, you‚Äôll instantly remember: that was the year I ___________________. When I look back on 2025, I‚Äôm going to think, that was the year I ran the original marathon in Greece. In a couple weeks, I‚Äôm going to try to do my Misogi in Rome. I‚Äôll let you know how it goes.¬Ý ‚Äì We are flooded with more information than entire civilizations could have produced, let alone imagined. And yet, how much of this matters? Or even holds up? When I was talking at Amazon, I brought up one of their company values which is ‚Äúfocus on the things that don‚Äôt change.‚Äù But how much of our information diet adheres to that? How much of what we give our attention to day to day, supports us in our goal to be long term thinkers?¬Ý ‚Äì Obviously I am biased as an author but I think most people should consume less news and read more books. Less news, more books. More history. More poetry. More literature. More philosophy. More myths. Because you know what those stories are about? They‚Äôre about what‚Äôs happening now! ‚Äì Almost every lesson you need to learn to deal with what‚Äôs happening in the world, almost every skill you need to develop to succeed in modern times is the topic of old art. I [...]
item.content:encoded:
Well, I‚Äôm now almost 40. Like officially almost 40. I woke up yesterday in Cle Elum, Washington, ran 20 miles and then turned 39. I did a little talk for Amazon and then flew home, getting in very late and very tired.¬Ý

I have been doing some version of this post for basically twenty years.¬Ý

(You can see my birthday posts for ‚Äã26‚Äã, ‚Äã27‚Äã, ‚Äã28‚Äã, ‚Äã29‚Äã, ‚Äã30‚Äã, ‚Äã31‚Äã, ‚Äã32, ‚Äã‚Äã33‚Äã, ‚Äã34‚Äã, ‚Äã35‚Äã, ‚Äã36‚Äã, 37, and 38‚Äã).¬Ý

How much have I learned and changed? Quite a bit, I‚Äôm proud to say. Not enough, I‚Äôd have to add much more honestly. I still fall prey to the same patterns. I am still doing too many of the same things. And worse, I‚Äôm still too often not aware of it until it‚Äôs painfully obvious much later. Which brings me to my first lesson for this year‚Äôs list.¬Ý

‚Äì At my talk in Seattle last week (I‚Äôm on tour this summer and fall if you want to come see me in the US and Australia), someone asked how I would describe myself in one word. I thought about it and I just said: ‚ÄúTrying.‚Äù I‚Äôm trying. Could I try a little harder? Would I like to make a little more progress? Yes. But I am trying.¬Ý

‚Äì I‚Äôve given hundreds of talks now, and I could give the same one every night, the same way, until I had it down cold. Instead, I try to change it each time‚ÄîI add things, I cut things, I work out new material right there on stage. If I have the opportunity, because of the audience or whatever, to give a totally new one that I haven‚Äôt done before, I see that as a gift not an obligation. Because harder is what makes me better. It also keeps life interesting.¬Ý

‚Äì One of the things that sneaks up on you as you get older are the blocks of time you can start measuring things in. For a while, four years‚Äîthe length of high school or college‚Äîis a measuring stick of something that takes a while. Then you get comfortable with things being a decade long‚Äî‚ÄúWe‚Äôve been friends of a decade‚Äù and ‚ÄúThat was like ten years ago?‚Äù‚Äîand then suddenly, it‚Äôs twenty years, like I did in the intro above. I had my twenty year high school reunion last year. This gives you perspective. You start to get a sense of how long things take and how much capacity you have to endure or live through.

‚Äì And yet even so, the passage of time can sneak up on you. I was looking at a baseball picture of my son the other day. It was him holding a bat, looking basically like the kid he is now, except it says 2025. How could he be old enough to at one point have been five years younger? I was trying to express to him how unbelievable that is to me. But it‚Äôs true. Tempus fugit the ancients said. Time flies. Be present. Be ready.¬Ý

‚Äì I don‚Äôt believe in time travel‚Ä¶but I do believe the work‚Äîlearning, research, exercise, etc‚Äîis a gift we give our future selves. Basically, you can‚Äôt make withdrawals from accounts you haven‚Äôt made deposits in.¬Ý¬Ý

‚Äì The purpose of any piece of writing at all is not the end product on the page. It‚Äôs the person YOU are on the other side of having done it. It‚Äôs the thinking long and hard about something. It‚Äôs the slow, tedious, difficult work of figuring out what you actually work. And the equally hard work of finding the words for what you think. AI can give you an essay, an article, a book, or a briefing. What it can‚Äôt give you is the person you can only become by doing the writing yourself.

‚Äì ‚ÄúIt is surprising,‚Äù Roosevelt said, ‚Äúhow much reading a man can do in time usually wasted.‚Äù That‚Äôs exactly right. Bring a book with you everywhere.¬Ý

‚Äì I don‚Äôt think I have ever taken a walk without thinking, after, ‚ÄúI am so glad I did that.‚Äù We rescued a dog last year and that‚Äôs been the best part. It‚Äôs ‚Äúforced‚Äù me to do something I love to do.¬Ý

‚Äì For fifteen years (again those chunks of time!) I‚Äôd had this idea sitting in the back of my head, and last July I finally did it: I ran the 26 miles from Marathon to Athens. On the Daily Stoic podcast, Jesse Itzler told me about this concept of the Misogi. Borrowed from an ancient Japanese purification ritual, the modern Misogi is about committing to one epic, year-defining challenge‚Äîsomething so significant, so hard, so memorable, that decades later, when you think back, you‚Äôll instantly remember: that was the year I ___________________. When I look back on 2025, I‚Äôm going to think, that was the year I ran the original marathon in Greece. In a couple weeks, I‚Äôm going to try to do my Misogi in Rome. I‚Äôll let you know how it goes.¬Ý

‚Äì We are flooded with more information than entire civilizations could have produced, let alone imagined. And yet, how much of this matters? Or even holds up? When I was talking at Amazon, I brought up one of their company values which is ‚Äúfocus on the things that don‚Äôt change.‚Äù But how much of our information diet adheres to that? How much of what we give our attention to day to day, supports us in our goal to be long term thinkers?¬Ý

‚Äì Obviously I am biased as an author but I think most people should consume less news and read more books. Less news, more books. More history. More poetry. More literature. More philosophy. More myths. Because you know what those stories are about? They‚Äôre about what‚Äôs happening now!

‚Äì Almost every lesson you need to learn to deal with what‚Äôs happening in the world, almost every skill you need to develop to succeed in modern times is the topic of old art. I was bowled over last summer when I read Euripides‚Äô The Children of Hercules. It‚Äôs a play from 430BC and I‚Äôd never even heard of it. In it, the young children of Hercules are driven to the Temple of Apollo in Marathon by a bounty hunter from an angry king, who demands they be handed over to be punished. ‚ÄúThey are suppliants and strangers,‚Äù the Athenians reply, ‚ÄúWho look to our city for help. / To reject them is to defy the gods.‚Äù The people of Marathon have to decide. In theory they care about the plight of the refugee, they know what their values say to do. But how much are they willing to risk for strangers? Is their compassion a match for the animus and anger of the fearful and the angry? Anyway, as Truman famously said, ‚Äúthe only thing new in the world is the history you don‚Äôt know.‚Äù

‚Äì Again, supreme irony: AI, this cutting-edge technology, actually makes the oldest skills more valuable than ever. Reading. Thinking. Knowing things. Empathy. Having taste. Understanding context. Detecting lies or nonsense.

‚Äì That last one is really important. The essential skill of our time is having a good bullshit detector. Can you spot an obvious agenda? Can you recognize legit craziness? Can steer clear of bad actors and grifters? I have been amazed at how bad people seem to be at this, especially people who host podcasts. C‚Äômon guys. Do better.¬Ý

‚Äì One of my favorite things to do each day is sit down and write the ‚ÄãDaily Dad email‚Äã. It‚Äôs one piece of wisdom‚Äîdrawn from history, science, literature, and other ordinary parents‚Äîthat goes out to about 100,000 parents around the world (sign up here). But really, I‚Äôm writing it for myself. Again, the purpose of any piece of writing is the person you are on the other side of having done it. We write to figure out what we think and believe. Which is something you can‚Äôt have someone else do for you. The point is to do it..¬Ý

‚Äì You‚Äôre going to look back on this period and wish you did more. I don‚Äôt mean work. I mean you‚Äôre going to wish you were more active and outspoken. That you went against the political current, that you lent a hand, that you participated. This fever will break (or rather, it will be broken) and we will all be judged. We will judge ourselves.¬Ý

‚Äì Related: I want to quote again some advice I got last year when I was weighing whether to say something I knew might cause me some problems. It came from a four star admiral: ‚ÄúI think you should just speak directly about what you truly believe. That is always a path.‚Äù

‚Äì And related even more: When you do this, you are doing the world a service. Courage is a team sport. Justice is a team sport. People are far more likely to stand up, to say ‚Äúthis is wrong,‚Äù when they’ve seen someone else do it first. So when you speak up, you‚Äôre not just taking a stand. When you are unapologetically yourself, you‚Äôre not just being yourself. You’re making it easier for the next person to.¬Ý

‚Äì Everyone could use a support group. A circle of people who‚Äôve been there, who give you advice, who relate to what you‚Äôre going through. When my first son was born, I wanted that and couldn‚Äôt find it for dads, so I built one, Daily Dad Society. A little over a year in, the conversations in our monthly live calls have changed how I parent. (Dads, join us!)

‚Äì You might think you‚Äôre zooming out to see the big picture, but almost certainly you can open the aperture even more. I was talking to Senator and retired astronaut Mark Kelly on the Daily Stoic podcast about a line in Meditations where Marcus Aurelius is talking about looking at the city from above, trying to imagine what Earth looked like from the stars as a way to ‚Äúwash off the mud of life below.‚Äù He responded by saying, ‚ÄúNow imagine if Marcus knew that we were one galaxy in a universe of two trillion galaxies.‚Äù So good.¬Ý

‚Äì When we moved out to rural Texas in 2015, there was basically no cell phone service at our house or on our dirt road. We tried to fix it‚ÄîI even put a booster on the roof‚Äîbut it didn’t work. We could have switched providers, but that seemed like a pain. So we got used to it. For over ten years, when we were out and about at home, our phones couldn’t make calls or send texts. It was almost predictable: I’d be back somewhere on my property and some incredibly important call would come through with just enough service for me to answer and not be able to talk. That‚Äôs why it snuck up on me the last couple months that my phone had full reception when I was out checking on the cows. Or walking the dog. Or even out in the garage. An inconvenience that had been part of our lives for so long just suddenly went away. And it took me some time to notice it. I guess that‚Äôs my point: Life doesn‚Äôt always get worse. It often gets better‚Ä¶and we don‚Äôt even notice.¬Ý

‚Äì A lot of people read, not enough people re-read. Don‚Äôt just read books, re-read books. There‚Äôs a great line the Stoics loved‚Äîthat we never step in the same river twice. The books don‚Äôt change, but you do. I re-read The Road and The Great Gatsby (there‚Äôs an incredible new annotated 100th-anniversary edition that came out last year with tons of fascinating asides that provide a ton of new context) this year. Both hit me very hard, which of course, is what the best literature does.¬Ý

‚Äì How well do those first two sentences in Gatsby hold up by the way?

In my younger and more vulnerable years my father gave me some advice that I‚Äôve been turning over in my mind ever since. ‚ÄúWhenever you feel like criticizing any one,‚Äù he told me, ‚Äújust remember that all the people in this world haven‚Äôt had the advantages that you‚Äôve had.‚Äù

‚Äì And for The Road (which I did a long interview for The New York Times about)? The idea of ‚Äòcarrying the fire‚Äô / being ‚Äòone of the good guys‚Äô‚Äîthat‚Äôs what life, what parenting, what making it through this crazy period of history is all about. As I quoted Stefan Zweig in last year‚Äôs birthday post: we must ‚Äúremain human in an inhuman time.‚Äù

‚Äì The novelist Philipp Meyer (whose book ‚Äã‚ÄãThe Son‚Äã‚Äã is an incredible read) told me on the podcast, ‚ÄúYou have to be very careful about what‚Äîand to whom‚Äîyou‚Äôre giving the best part of your day.‚Äù Wake up, scroll, check email, meetings, busy work, whatever‚Äîwhy do we hand the best part of our day to other people’s emergencies and agendas? I fiercely protect my mornings‚Äîfamily, exercise then writing. I want to give my best self to my most important things.

‚Äì Where do your own emotional patterns get in the way of clear thinking? When you‚Äôre hurt or betrayed or unexpectedly challenged, pay attention to how you react. Notice the ‚Äúage‚Äù of that reaction. Is it mature, measured, proportional? Or does it feel more like a wounded eight-year-old lashing out? That‚Äôs your inner child‚Äîthe pain you still carry from early experiences, hijacking your adult mind.

‚Äì To go to Euripides‚Äô point about the refugees and the weak: There is no such thing as an issue that doesn‚Äôt affect you. People thought the crack epidemic was an urban issue‚Ä¶if they‚Äôd responded with more kindness and support (and solutions instead of punishment and fear), perhaps the country would have been better equipped to deal with the opioid epidemic. We will come to think the same about many other things in the future‚Ä¶

‚Äì Something that‚Äôs happened with Daily Stoic over the years is as it has grown, so has the number of copycats. And so we‚Äôre constantly asking, what can only we do? What can only we write? What can only we create? Don‚Äôt be like everyone else. Be yourself! That‚Äôs where you have a monopoly. That‚Äôs something Iron Maiden‚Äôs manager once said‚Äîthat he wasn‚Äôt in the ‚Äúmusic business‚Äù as some people thought, he was in the ‚ÄúIron fucking Maiden business.‚Äù¬Ý

‚Äì The reward for success should not be that you don‚Äôt get to practice your craft anymore. If power gives you less power over your schedule and your day, is it actually power?

‚Äì When we were working on ‚Äã‚Äã‚ÄãWhat You‚Äôre Made For‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã, the late George Raveling‚Äîwho I think is one of the most remarkable people of the second half of the 20th century‚Äîtold me that when he became Nike‚Äôs Director of International Basketball at 63, having no prior corporate experience, he was overwhelmed by self-doubt. Until a mentor gave him a simple system: ‚ÄúWhen you leave the office every day, leave a yellow pad in the middle of the desk, and when you come in the morning, write down the three most important things you gotta get done that day in that order. That day, do not do anything else but the first thing on the pad. And if you get the first one, then you go to the second one.‚Äù If you just did that, how much better would you be? How much better would the world be if people could consistently do just that?¬Ý

‚Äì I‚Äôve been keeping a One Line a Day journal for nine years now (you should keep one, they‚Äôre amazing). It’s weird how much you don’t remember, even of your own life. But my favorite part of the journal is when things line up. This day last year, I was working on my next book which starts in media res. Today, I sat down to work on the book and I have almost come back to the starting point. I love that. Half way left!¬Ý

‚Äì As I‚Äôve made some money over the years, I‚Äôve tried some expensive things. I‚Äôve done the things that people use their money to do‚Äîwhether it‚Äôs upgrading travel or living conditions or hiring help. Most of the time, it‚Äôs honestly not worth the money. Or it‚Äôs not as great as you think it‚Äôs going to be.¬Ý

‚Äì The one exception‚Äîin the last two years, my kids ended up at a very expensive school that‚Äôs specialized for their needs. I will say, that‚Äôs maybe the one thing where I really felt like I was getting what I paid for. Would I love it if their local public schools could do that (since via my taxes I am also paying for that)? Yes, of course. And maybe, hopefully that will change in the future. But right now it reminds me of the Marcus Aurelius quote about what he learned from his great-grandfather, ‚Äúto hire good private teachers, and to accept the resulting costs as money well-spent.‚Äù

‚Äì The antidote to shame/guilt, I was told, is total accountability.¬Ý

‚Äì You think the rich and powerful have it all. But they‚Äôre some of the most jealous people alive‚Äînot just of each other, but of ordinary people with freedom, with time, with happiness. And you can have those things easily. In fact, you may well have them right now. You do not need to strike it rich‚Ä¶you were born rich. You do not need to climb to the top‚Ä¶you can simply step off the treadmill. Or at least some of them.¬Ý¬Ý

‚Äì I said ‚ÄòNo‚Äô to a pretty big opportunity over the summer. There were a bunch of reasons I passed but the one I kept thinking of was ‚ÄúWas this exciting but ultimately selfish choice the thing that blew up my family?‚Äù I would like to stay married. I like to be able to pick my kids up from school‚Äînot be away from home many nights in a row. So in the end, the choice was clear. The point is: You have to know what you like about your life to be able to evaluate the opportunities that arise.¬Ý

‚Äì There‚Äôs a scene in Mad Men where a young copywriter who’s grown to hate Don Draper corners him in the elevator: ‚ÄúI feel bad for you.‚Äù Draper just looks at him and says, ‚ÄúI don’t think about you at all.‚Äù It‚Äôs a great line especially because Don Draper was horrible and did horrible things to people! The people who take up the most space in our heads usually aren’t thinking about us. They aren‚Äôt being awful to torture us. There‚Äôs nothing personal in it. And we are adding insult to injury when we let them consume our sanity and happiness.¬Ý

‚Äì How many times have I heard The Smashing Pumpkins‚Äô Tonight Tonight? Hundreds easily. I don‚Äôt know why it wasn‚Äôt until a couple weeks ago, when it came on randomly in a playlist, that that lyric hit me.¬Ý

Time is never time at all¬Ý

You can never ever leave¬Ý

Without leaving a piece of youth

‚Äì Not just our youth, but our middle age and our golden years. Our children‚Äôs youth, the best years of a marriage, of a career, of anything. There is a cost to everything we do, everything we agree to, every commitment we make. We have to spend our time wisely because we are paying for it with our lives.¬Ý

That‚Äôs the message I always give a little extra consideration on my birthdays, but it‚Äôs a thought that shouldn‚Äôt ever be far from our minds.¬Ý

Memento Mori.

item.title: This Is Something You Have To Get Good At
item.dc:creator: Ryan Holiday
item.description: I‚Äôll tell you, very few people become writers because they want to stand up in front of a room full of strangers and talk. Often, in fact, it‚Äôs the opposite. What draws most people to writing in the first place is that they‚Äôre introverts. At least, that‚Äôs what drew me! We like to sit quietly, alone in a room, working through our thoughts on the page. That‚Äôs the whole appeal. So one of the strange ironies of having any success as a writer is that you are then invited to do the opposite of that. To leave your quiet room. To get up on a stage. To talk in front of people.¬Ý It was actually before my first book even came out that I got my first invitation to talk at an international marketing conference. And I remember thinking: this is precisely the thing I became a writer to avoid. But over time, I started to feel differently about it. Partly because they weren‚Äôt just inviting but offering to pay, but also because I could see that it was a way of reaching people that really matters. People don‚Äôt just want to read the things you‚Äôve written and shaped on the page. They want to hear you talk about them. They want it straight from the horse‚Äôs mouth. Think about the State of the Union Address. For over a century, the president didn‚Äôt deliver it as a speech‚Äîit was just a written report sent over to Congress, the ‚ÄúPresident‚Äôs Annual Message.‚Äù But eventually, people realized there are things you just can‚Äôt get from a letter. They wanted to hear the president say it. To watch him explain it. To hear which words he stressed and which he didn‚Äôt. And then there‚Äôs the fact that public speaking is just part of life. There is no line of work where this is actually avoidable. An entrepreneur has to get up and pitch investors. A coach has to get up and address the team. A professor has to deliver lectures. A salesperson stands before a skeptical group of buyers. A freelancer has to sell themselves to a potential client. An athlete has to talk to the press. A friend has to get up and give a wedding toast. My nine year old and six year old just had their school‚Äôs annual showcase. We will all have to leave our comfort zone from time to time. Yet many of us would almost rather die than do this. The comedian Jerry Seinfeld once noted that people rank public speaking as worse than the fear of death, which means, quite insanely, that at a funeral the average person would rather be in the casket than delivering the eulogy. In Courage Is Calling, I tell the story of Crassus. In ancient Rome, there was perhaps no better orator than Crassus, famed for his brilliant speeches and prosecutions of the corrupt and the evil. At least that‚Äôs how he appeared to his audiences. You would not have known, as he later admitted, that at the outset of every speech he would ‚Äúfeel a tremor through my whole thoughts, as it were, and limbs.‚Äù Even as a master, he still experienced doubt‚Äîstill felt waves of overwhelming anxiety and fear crash over him before he went onstage. At the beginning of his career, it was even worse. He recounts his eternal debt and gratitude to a judge who, at one of Crassus‚Äôs first public appearances, could tell how ‚Äúabsolutely disheartened and incapacitated with fear‚Äù the boy was, and adjourned the hearing until a later date. We can imagine those merciful words from the judge, sparing Crassus as he no doubt prayed he would be spared, as we have prayed a thousand times, second only to his hope that he might be struck down and killed rather than have to go on. Yet we would not be talking about Crassus had he not cultivated the ability to push through that fear. There are unfortunately no tricks to cultivating this ability. You can‚Äôt read your way to it. You can‚Äôt think your way to it. You just gotta do it. You make the pitch. You get up on the stage. You deliver the pre-game speech. You give the toast. And then you do it again and again.¬Ý That‚Äôs what I was telling my sons and one of their nervous classmates. I get stage fright too. It is scary to get up there. But it gets less scary the more you do it. Confidence is built by competence, there‚Äôs no way around it.¬Ý Come see me take the stage to talk Stoicism on the Daily Stoic Live tour‚Äîgrab tickets here! There‚Äôs another irony in all this. The philosophy I‚Äôve spent my life writing about isn‚Äôt really a written philosophy at all. Because so much of Stoicism comes down to us in books, it‚Äôs easy to think of the Stoics as writers. Isn‚Äôt that how we got Meditations? Or Seneca‚Äôs Letters? Yes. But that‚Äôs not how we got the philosophy itself. Stoicism began on a porch in Athens‚Äîthe Stoa Poikile, the ‚ÄúPainted Porch‚Äù‚Äîwhere Zeno would talk and trade ideas with whoever happened to be around. The playwright David Mamet had a good way of putting it: what he loves about the Stoics is that they were just ‚Äúporch guys.‚Äù Regular people, hanging out, talking about how to become the best version of themselves. Cato, the man widely admired as the greatest Stoic of them all‚Äîthere isn‚Äôt even a secondhand record of his words. We know that he liked to do his philosophizing on foot. Plutarch tells us he would take meandering walks through Rome, talking with whoever he met ‚Äúon his rounds.‚Äù And for all his austere habits, his other practice was hosting philosophical dinners, where he talked about ideas long into the night. Epictetus never wrote anything down. His Discourses comes from a student, who tells us that ‚Äúwhatever I used to hear him say I wrote down, word for word, as [...]
item.content:encoded:
I‚Äôll tell you, very few people become writers because they want to stand up in front of a room full of strangers and talk.

Often, in fact, it‚Äôs the opposite. What draws most people to writing in the first place is that they‚Äôre introverts. At least, that‚Äôs what drew me! We like to sit quietly, alone in a room, working through our thoughts on the page. That‚Äôs the whole appeal.

So one of the strange ironies of having any success as a writer is that you are then invited to do the opposite of that. To leave your quiet room. To get up on a stage. To talk in front of people.¬Ý

It was actually before my first book even came out that I got my first invitation to talk at an international marketing conference. And I remember thinking: this is precisely the thing I became a writer to avoid.

But over time, I started to feel differently about it. Partly because they weren‚Äôt just inviting but offering to pay, but also because I could see that it was a way of reaching people that really matters. People don‚Äôt just want to read the things you‚Äôve written and shaped on the page. They want to hear you talk about them. They want it straight from the horse‚Äôs mouth. Think about the State of the Union Address. For over a century, the president didn‚Äôt deliver it as a speech‚Äîit was just a written report sent over to Congress, the ‚ÄúPresident‚Äôs Annual Message.‚Äù But eventually, people realized there are things you just can‚Äôt get from a letter. They wanted to hear the president say it. To watch him explain it. To hear which words he stressed and which he didn‚Äôt.

And then there‚Äôs the fact that public speaking is just part of life. There is no line of work where this is actually avoidable. An entrepreneur has to get up and pitch investors. A coach has to get up and address the team. A professor has to deliver lectures. A salesperson stands before a skeptical group of buyers. A freelancer has to sell themselves to a potential client. An athlete has to talk to the press. A friend has to get up and give a wedding toast. My nine year old and six year old just had their school‚Äôs annual showcase.

We will all have to leave our comfort zone from time to time. Yet many of us would almost rather die than do this. The comedian Jerry Seinfeld once noted that people rank public speaking as worse than the fear of death, which means, quite insanely, that at a funeral the average person would rather be in the casket than delivering the eulogy.

In Courage Is Calling, I tell the story of Crassus. In ancient Rome, there was perhaps no better orator than Crassus, famed for his brilliant speeches and prosecutions of the corrupt and the evil. At least that‚Äôs how he appeared to his audiences. You would not have known, as he later admitted, that at the outset of every speech he would ‚Äúfeel a tremor through my whole thoughts, as it were, and limbs.‚Äù Even as a master, he still experienced doubt‚Äîstill felt waves of overwhelming anxiety and fear crash over him before he went onstage.

At the beginning of his career, it was even worse. He recounts his eternal debt and gratitude to a judge who, at one of Crassus‚Äôs first public appearances, could tell how ‚Äúabsolutely disheartened and incapacitated with fear‚Äù the boy was, and adjourned the hearing until a later date. We can imagine those merciful words from the judge, sparing Crassus as he no doubt prayed he would be spared, as we have prayed a thousand times, second only to his hope that he might be struck down and killed rather than have to go on.

Yet we would not be talking about Crassus had he not cultivated the ability to push through that fear. There are unfortunately no tricks to cultivating this ability. You can‚Äôt read your way to it. You can‚Äôt think your way to it. You just gotta do it. You make the pitch. You get up on the stage. You deliver the pre-game speech. You give the toast. And then you do it again and again.¬Ý

That‚Äôs what I was telling my sons and one of their nervous classmates. I get stage fright too. It is scary to get up there. But it gets less scary the more you do it. Confidence is built by competence, there‚Äôs no way around it.¬Ý

Come see me take the stage to talk Stoicism on the Daily Stoic Live tour‚Äîgrab tickets here!

There‚Äôs another irony in all this. The philosophy I‚Äôve spent my life writing about isn‚Äôt really a written philosophy at all. Because so much of Stoicism comes down to us in books, it‚Äôs easy to think of the Stoics as writers.

Isn‚Äôt that how we got Meditations? Or Seneca‚Äôs Letters?

Yes. But that‚Äôs not how we got the philosophy itself.

Stoicism began on a porch in Athens‚Äîthe Stoa Poikile, the ‚ÄúPainted Porch‚Äù‚Äîwhere Zeno would talk and trade ideas with whoever happened to be around. The playwright David Mamet had a good way of putting it: what he loves about the Stoics is that they were just ‚Äúporch guys.‚Äù Regular people, hanging out, talking about how to become the best version of themselves.

Cato, the man widely admired as the greatest Stoic of them all‚Äîthere isn‚Äôt even a secondhand record of his words. We know that he liked to do his philosophizing on foot. Plutarch tells us he would take meandering walks through Rome, talking with whoever he met ‚Äúon his rounds.‚Äù And for all his austere habits, his other practice was hosting philosophical dinners, where he talked about ideas long into the night.

Epictetus never wrote anything down. His Discourses comes from a student, who tells us that ‚Äúwhatever I used to hear him say I wrote down, word for word, as best I could, as a record for later use of his thought and frank expression.‚Äù That‚Äôs how Epictetus taught‚Äîin person, going back and forth in real time.

So for most of its history, Stoicism was a spoken, conversational philosophy. It‚Äôs meant to be heard, meant to be talked about, meant for that back and forth. And this is a long, unbroken tradition that goes back thousands of years, one that continues to this day.

I‚Äôm thinking about all this because I happen to be right in the middle of it. As I write this, I‚Äôm preparing for the Daily Stoic Live tour. I‚Äôm going to be spending a good chunk of the rest of the year making the rounds, talking Stoicism all over the world: Portland (on Monday!), San Francisco (on June 11th), Chicago, Boston, and a bunch of other U.S. cities, before heading all the way to Australia and New Zealand in October. Night after night of overriding that introverted part of me that would rather just stay home and write.

Over the years, though, this is something I‚Äôve really tried to get good at. I‚Äôve done it hundreds of times now. In big venues and small venues. To American audiences and international audiences. With slides and without slides. With technology and with no technology. Long talks and short talks.¬Ý

And I‚Äôve come to see it as a meaningful and exciting way to share these ideas with people, but also as this high-wire act that forces me to get better. That‚Äôs why I don‚Äôt make it easy on myself. I could give the same talk every night, the same way, until I had it down cold. Instead, I‚Äôm changing it each time. I add things. I cut things. I try one delivery one night and a different one the next. I prepare some stuff I know works, and some new stuff to work out right there on stage.

Because that makes it harder. And harder is what makes me better.

So now‚Ä¶I‚Äôve got to get back to preparing to talk to rooms full of strangers. Hope to see you there!

item.title: My Office Explained in 13 Objects
item.dc:creator: Ryan Holiday
item.description: When I first moved in, six years ago. If you walk into the locker room or practice facility for a professional sports team or elite college program, one of the things that strikes you is what they put up on the wall.¬Ý The walls are tattooed with precepts. Every hallway and doorway is decorated with motivational quotes. There are statues of former players and coaches, the legends who set the standard. There are framed photographs of the greatest moments in the team‚Äôs history. Why do they do this? Because reminders are powerful. They make you better. They give you something to hold up and try to live up to each day. They turn the habits, standards, or values you‚Äôre trying to hold yourself to into something more than an idea, and that helps‚Äîa lot. I do something similar in my office. It‚Äôs filled with little knicknacks and pictures. Reminders and totems. Things I‚Äôve collected over the years that mean something to me. Objects that represent who I‚Äôm trying to be, what I‚Äôm trying to do, how I want to work and live. Here are 13 of them‚Äîand what they‚Äôre there to remind me of. 1. A ‚ÄúNo‚Äù Sign A few years ago, Dr. Jonathan Fader, a sports psychologist who spent nearly a decade with the New York Mets, gave me this picture of Oliver Sacks in his office. Behind Sacks, who is speaking on the phone, is a large sign that just says, ‚ÄúNO!‚Äù I have it hanging between two pictures of my kids‚Äîa reminder that when I say no, I am saying YES to them.¬Ý I have that photo hanging on the wall in my office, just a few feet from a couple of Truman memos. One, from 1969, politely explains his long-standing policy not to answer questions from every random person who contacts him‚Äîhe was eighty-five, still working, sensing his time was limited. The other is a brief reply telling someone that their question would be answered in detail in his next book. But the one I like the most has Truman‚Äôs handwriting on it. Shortly after he became president, Truman was invited to the fifth annual Roosevelt Day in Chicago. His secretary wrote an inner-office memo to ask if they should start saying no to these sorts of requests with all the demands he had on his schedule. ‚ÄúThe proper answer underlined, HST‚Äù, he wrote back. I once heard someone say that early in our careers, we say yes to everything so that one day we can afford to say no. As I‚Äôve been lucky enough to succeed as a writer, I‚Äôve watched my inbound requests skyrocket. It is literally impossible to even read, let alone reply, to everyone and everything. Life is about tradeoffs. It demands that we be a little selfish. It requires that we tune out and tune in. Otherwise we can‚Äôt do what we do. The photos and letters are my constant reminders that everything I say yes to is taking me away from my family who I‚Äôve already promised most of my time to. And from my writing, which is not only the thing that is most meaningful to me and how I make my actual living, but it‚Äôs how I can help the most people. So if I say yes to one random person, I‚Äôm saying no to a lot more people by taking that time and energy away from my writing. 2. Books. Books. Books. This isn‚Äôt one object. It‚Äôs hundreds of them. Thousands, actually. I have books lining the walls, stacked on the floor, piled on my desk, crammed into every available surface. It’s been said that a house without books is not a home. Cicero famously said that all that was needed for a happy life was a library and a garden. Aristotle filled his house with so many books that Plato nicknamed it ‚Äúthe house of the reader.‚Äù I don‚Äôt know if my office qualifies as a proper library, but it‚Äôs close. And there‚Äôs a reason for that. I want to be surrounded by the best thinking of the last few thousand years. I want history‚Äôs greatest minds within arm‚Äôs reach. I want to be able to look up from my desk and see the spines of books that have shaped how I think, how I write, how I live. In Wisdom Takes Work, I talk about how books allow us to talk to the dead. A shelf full of books allows us to create a room full of mentors, waiting patiently to be consulted. They don‚Äôt demand anything. They just sit there, ready, until you need them. That‚Äôs the kind of company I want to keep. 3. My ‚ÄúWhy‚Äù This notecard is tapped to the wall next to my desk I‚Äôve been very lucky. I did not think when I started writing The Obstacle is the Way more than ten years ago that it would sell nearly as well as it did or that I would have anything close to the success‚Äîor platform‚Äîthat I‚Äôve somehow found myself with. I loved the ideas in Stoicism and was just trying to talk to people about them. I have no formal training or expertise, so I had no idea that I would somehow be identified with them, or seen as a representative of them.¬Ý What this means to me then, as I look at the fact that the books have sold millions of copies and accumulated millions of followers, is that I have a certain responsibility and obligation. I did not invent this philosophy. It is not mine. So yes, writing books is a business. My bookstore, The Painted Porch, is a business. Daily Stoic is a business. But I always try to ask myself not if I am making good business decisions, but am I being a good steward of Stoicism, of the philosophy that‚Äôs given so much to me? Am I being honest and ethical and fair and reasonable and moderate? That question guides everything we [...]
item.content:encoded:
When I first moved in, six years ago.

If you walk into the locker room or practice facility for a professional sports team or elite college program, one of the things that strikes you is what they put up on the wall.¬Ý

The walls are tattooed with precepts. Every hallway and doorway is decorated with motivational quotes. There are statues of former players and coaches, the legends who set the standard. There are framed photographs of the greatest moments in the team‚Äôs history.

Why do they do this?

Because reminders are powerful. They make you better. They give you something to hold up and try to live up to each day. They turn the habits, standards, or values you‚Äôre trying to hold yourself to into something more than an idea, and that helps‚Äîa lot.

I do something similar in my office. It‚Äôs filled with little knicknacks and pictures. Reminders and totems. Things I‚Äôve collected over the years that mean something to me. Objects that represent who I‚Äôm trying to be, what I‚Äôm trying to do, how I want to work and live.

Here are 13 of them‚Äîand what they‚Äôre there to remind me of.

1. A ‚ÄúNo‚Äù Sign

A few years ago, Dr. Jonathan Fader, a sports psychologist who spent nearly a decade with the New York Mets, gave me this picture of Oliver Sacks in his office. Behind Sacks, who is speaking on the phone, is a large sign that just says, ‚ÄúNO!‚Äù I have it hanging between two pictures of my kids‚Äîa reminder that when I say no, I am saying YES to them.¬Ý

I have that photo hanging on the wall in my office, just a few feet from a couple of Truman memos. One, from 1969, politely explains his long-standing policy not to answer questions from every random person who contacts him‚Äîhe was eighty-five, still working, sensing his time was limited. The other is a brief reply telling someone that their question would be answered in detail in his next book. But the one I like the most has Truman‚Äôs handwriting on it. Shortly after he became president, Truman was invited to the fifth annual Roosevelt Day in Chicago. His secretary wrote an inner-office memo to ask if they should start saying no to these sorts of requests with all the demands he had on his schedule. ‚ÄúThe proper answer underlined, HST‚Äù, he wrote back.

I once heard someone say that early in our careers, we say yes to everything so that one day we can afford to say no. As I‚Äôve been lucky enough to succeed as a writer, I‚Äôve watched my inbound requests skyrocket. It is literally impossible to even read, let alone reply, to everyone and everything.

Life is about tradeoffs. It demands that we be a little selfish. It requires that we tune out and tune in. Otherwise we can‚Äôt do what we do.

The photos and letters are my constant reminders that everything I say yes to is taking me away from my family who I‚Äôve already promised most of my time to. And from my writing, which is not only the thing that is most meaningful to me and how I make my actual living, but it‚Äôs how I can help the most people. So if I say yes to one random person, I‚Äôm saying no to a lot more people by taking that time and energy away from my writing.

2. Books. Books. Books.

This isn‚Äôt one object. It‚Äôs hundreds of them. Thousands, actually. I have books lining the walls, stacked on the floor, piled on my desk, crammed into every available surface.

It’s been said that a house without books is not a home. Cicero famously said that all that was needed for a happy life was a library and a garden. Aristotle filled his house with so many books that Plato nicknamed it ‚Äúthe house of the reader.‚Äù

I don‚Äôt know if my office qualifies as a proper library, but it‚Äôs close. And there‚Äôs a reason for that. I want to be surrounded by the best thinking of the last few thousand years. I want history‚Äôs greatest minds within arm‚Äôs reach. I want to be able to look up from my desk and see the spines of books that have shaped how I think, how I write, how I live.

In Wisdom Takes Work, I talk about how books allow us to talk to the dead. A shelf full of books allows us to create a room full of mentors, waiting patiently to be consulted.

They don‚Äôt demand anything. They just sit there, ready, until you need them. That‚Äôs the kind of company I want to keep.

3. My ‚ÄúWhy‚Äù

This notecard is tapped to the wall next to my desk

I‚Äôve been very lucky. I did not think when I started writing The Obstacle is the Way more than ten years ago that it would sell nearly as well as it did or that I would have anything close to the success‚Äîor platform‚Äîthat I‚Äôve somehow found myself with. I loved the ideas in Stoicism and was just trying to talk to people about them. I have no formal training or expertise, so I had no idea that I would somehow be identified with them, or seen as a representative of them.¬Ý

What this means to me then, as I look at the fact that the books have sold millions of copies and accumulated millions of followers, is that I have a certain responsibility and obligation. I did not invent this philosophy. It is not mine. So yes, writing books is a business. My bookstore, The Painted Porch, is a business. Daily Stoic is a business. But I always try to ask myself not if I am making good business decisions, but am I being a good steward of Stoicism, of the philosophy that‚Äôs given so much to me? Am I being honest and ethical and fair and reasonable and moderate?

That question guides everything we do. How we spend our time and money. The content we make. The decision to stop spending money on advertising. The causes we support. The injustices we speak out about. Not all of these decisions are good for business. Some are not popular. Some make people mad. Some have probably cost us business.¬Ý

But the filter isn‚Äôt if I am making good business decisions. It‚Äôs if I am being a good steward of Stoicism, of the philosophy that‚Äôs given so much to me. Am I being honest and ethical and fair and reasonable and moderate? Am I using the success that this philosophy has brought me to introduce more people to the philosophy? That’s my goal.

4. A Grammy?

Years ago, I was an associate producer on a jazz album that won a Grammy in 2017. I got to walk the red carpet and go onstage and accept it. It was pretty surreal. Afterwards, they gave us a paper certificate instead of actual trophies for everyone involved. So I had this one made with a special inscription on it:¬Ý

When you die, this will go in the trash alongside all your other ‚Äúaccomplishments‚Äù

It’s a reminder that awards, bestseller lists, medals, championship rings‚Äînone of it really matters. They’re just pieces of metal, bits of glass, plaques on a wall. Stuff that will end up in an estate sale or a box in an attic or a landfill.

In the end, what matters is who you were as a person. Did you do your best while you were in the game? Did you leave anything on the table creatively? Did you phone it in or did you give it everything you had?

That’s what matters. Everything else is garbage.

5. Joan Didion‚Äôs Chair
