Speaking of estate sales‚Ä¶

After Joan Didion died, her estate auctioned off some of her belongings, and I bought her writing chair.

Last year, I was sitting in it, working on a chapter in Wisdom Takes Work about the importance of keeping a commonplace book. I pulled out my notecards and found an old, worn card referencing something Didion had written about notebooks in her book Slouching Towards Bethlehem. I walked over to the shelf, pulled it down, and there it was‚Äîa beautiful essay called ‚ÄúOn Keeping a Notebook,‚Äù written in 1966.

I got goosebumps. Not just because it was exactly what I needed, but because I was sitting, at that very moment, in her chair. How did I know, nine years ago when I first read that book, when I took the time to jot that little reference on a notecard, that it would be of use to future-me?

‚ÄúWhy did I write it down?‚Äù Didion asks in that essay. ‚ÄúIn order to remember, of course, but exactly what was it I wanted to remember?‚Äù

Anyways, Didion was, like me, from Sacramento. And when I see her chair, I think about where I‚Äôm from and where it‚Äôs possible to go. I think about how I’m not alone in this work. How I‚Äôm part of a long tradition of writers and thinkers and note-takers stretching back centuries. How the craft has been handed down, and it‚Äôs my job to honor it.

6. Notecards and Notecards and Notecards

And speaking of the importance of keeping a commonplace book‚Ä¶

I think everyone should have a commonplace book, or, to use a more modern term, a second brain‚Äîa collection of ideas, quotes, observations, and information gathered over time. (Here‚Äôs a video on my commonplace book method).

Two thousand years ago, Pliny the elder said, ‚ÄúNever read without taking extracts.‚Äù Seneca put it another way: ‚ÄúWe should hunt out the helpful pieces of teaching and the spirited and noble-minded sayings which are capable of immediate practical application‚Äînot far far-fetched or archaic expressions or extravagant metaphors and figures of speech‚Äîand learn them so well that words become works.‚Äù

That‚Äôs why I keep blank notecards everywhere. I‚Äôm constantly capturing quotes, stories, ideas, observations‚Äîanything that strikes me.¬Ý

Whether we‚Äôre beginning some creative work or we‚Äôre trying to solve some complex problem, we should never be starting from zero. Invariably, at some point in our lives, we have seen or read or heard something that would be of use in this situation. But will we remember it? Will we have access to it?

It happens to me all the time. I‚Äôm working on something, stuck, not sure where to go next‚Äîand I reach into a box and pull out a notecard I wrote five or ten years ago, and it‚Äôs exactly what I needed. And every time this happens, I‚Äôm grateful I took the few seconds to write the thing down.

7. The Four Virtues¬Ý

I mentioned earlier that sports facilities are filled with reminders‚Äîsigns and symbols placed in spots where players can‚Äôt miss them. Above the tunnel. Outside the weight room. On the way to the field.

On the wall coming down the stairs from my office, I had the Four Virtues logo painted on the wall. Every time I leave to go down into to the bookstore or out into the world, I pass it.

Courage. Temperance. Justice. Wisdom.

The ‚Äútouchstones of goodness,‚Äù Marcus Aurelius called them. They‚Äôre known as the cardinal virtues‚Äînot because they come down from church authorities, C. S. Lewis pointed out, but because the word comes from the Latin cardo, meaning hinge. They‚Äôre pivotal. The stuff that the door to the good life hangs on.

There’s a reason the four points on a compass are called the cardinal directions. North, south, east, west‚Äîthese four virtues are a kind of compass (there‚Äôs a reason that the four points on a compass are called the ‚Äúcardinal directions‚Äù).¬Ý

They guide me. They show me where I am and where I’m trying to go.

8. First Draft Print

The famous Hemingway line on writing helps me through every book.

I‚Äôm in the middle of my next book right now, for instance. It‚Äôs natural to instinctively compare it to the ones that have been published. But that‚Äôs not a helpful comparison, and it‚Äôs not a fair comparison. Precisely zero of those other books were immediately accepted by my publisher. Every one of them went through countless drafts to get to where they needed to be.

So I can only compare this book I‚Äôm in the middle of against the middle of my other books‚Äînot what I eventually published. It‚Äôs helpful to have the constant reminder: every book‚Äînot just mine‚Äîlooks and feels clumsy and awkward and imperfect at this point.

Precisely zero of my sixteen books were immediately accepted by my publisher‚Äîand they were right to kick them back at me. In being forced to go back to the manuscript, I got the books to where they needed to be.

9. A Very Special Pinecone

When I was reading John Vaillant‚Äôs book Fire Weather (he also wrote The Tiger), I learned about this conifer tree I‚Äôd never heard of before.¬Ý

It‚Äôs like any other pine tree: it drops its pinecone and the pinecone is what generates the next generation of trees. Except this species is different. The cone is sealed shut with resin, locking the seeds inside. It only opens up‚Äîonly looks like the one I‚Äôm holding in the picture above‚Äîif it‚Äôs exposed to temperatures that the climate does not naturally reach. It‚Äôs only a forest fire, which seems destructive and merciless and awful, that can unlock this tree‚Äôs ability to spawn and grow.¬Ý

I have a few of these pinecones sitting on my desk. To me, they‚Äôre an illustration of the idea that the obstacle is the way. Unless we are exposed to difficulty and stress and adversity and situations that we did not want, we can‚Äôt fully become what we‚Äôre capable of becoming. We can‚Äôt fully unlock our potential.

10. Me, But Younger

On the back cover of Anthony Hopkins‚Äô memoir (which, to my disbelief, I was mentioned in), there‚Äôs a photograph of him at three or four years old, on a beach with his father. Looking back at that photo eighty years later, Hopkins said, ‚ÄúI look now at my life, and I think, ‚ÄòWe did ok, kid. We did ok.‚Äô‚Äù

That‚Äôs part of why I keep a few photos of younger me in my office. To remember to step back from time to time to see how far I‚Äôve come. To look at that kid and say, We‚Äôre doing alright. We‚Äôre doing alright.

There‚Äôs another reason too.

In Stillness is the Key, I wrote about inner child work‚Äîthe practice of staying aware of the younger version of yourself that still lives inside you. When you’re hurt or betrayed or unexpectedly challenged, pay attention to how you react. Notice the ‚Äúage‚Äù of that reaction. Is it mature, measured, proportional? Or does it feel more like a wounded eight-year-old lashing out?

That‚Äôs your inner child‚Äîthe pain you still carry from early experiences, hijacking your adult mind.

The photos help me stay aware of him. To notice when he’s the one reacting instead of me. To take care of him rather than let him run the show.

11. ‚ÄúSense of Urgency‚Äù Signs

Preparation is important.

Planning is important.

Reflection is important.

I mean, I wrote a whole book on this because it‚Äôs true.¬Ý

At the same time, I put up two signs at The Daily Stoic offices and in the backstock of The Painted Porch that say, ‚ÄúSense of Urgency.‚Äù

It‚Äôs something I cribbed from the kitchens of Thomas Keller, the creator of Per Se. He wanted his staff to understand that they weren‚Äôt waiting on customers‚Ä¶the customers were quite literally waiting for them. Sure, making great food takes time and it can‚Äôt be rushed‚Ä¶but it also can‚Äôt be slow-walked.

There is an old Latin expression that I think captures the balance here nicely: Festina lente. Make haste slowly. A sense of urgency‚Ä¶with a purpose. Energy plus moderation. Measured exertion. Eagerness, with control.¬Ý

That, to me, is what the ‚ÄúSense of Urgency‚Äù sign is a reminder of‚Äîit‚Äôs about getting things done, properly and consistently.

12. Chunk of an Old Tombstone

I‚Äôve talked before about the Memento Mori coin‚ÄîI keep one on my desk and carry another in my pocket. On the front it has a rendering of Champaigne‚Äôs Still Life with a Skull painting. On the back, it has Marcus Aurelius‚Äôs quote: ‚ÄúYou could leave life right now. Let that determine what you do and say and think.‚Äù Except I cut off the last part‚Äîas a reminder that there isn‚Äôt even time to go through the whole quote.¬Ý

I also have this chunk of an old Victorian tombstone. How it left that cemetery and came to be for sale, I don‚Äôt know and don‚Äôt want to know.¬Ý

But I know that it sobers me and sets me right each time I look at it. Because the piece had just one word on it. It says, ‚ÄúDad.‚Äù

Somebody who so identified with that word they wanted it on their tombstone; who lived and died and whose gravestone eventually even fell into disrepair. Who were they? How did they pass? Are they missed? Were they famous? It doesn‚Äôt matter. They are gone now. Almost certainly, they were gone too soon. They left behind a family. They will never walk or speak or love or cry again.¬Ý

And so it will go for me. And so it will go for you.¬Ý

13. My Guiding Sentences

On every project I do, I print out a notecard with a sentence or an admonition that captures the essence of what I am trying to achieve on that project.¬Ý

When I was working on Ego is the Enemy, I had this quote from Machiavelli on the wall to inspire its style and ethos: ‚ÄúI have not adorned this work with fine phrases, with swelling, pompous words, or with any of those blandishments or external ornaments with which many set forth and decorate their matter. For I have chosen either that nothing at all should bring it honor or that the variety of its material and the gravity of its subject matter alone should make it welcome.‚Äù

When I was working on the Stoic Virtues series, I had a quote from Martha Graham: ‚ÄúNever be afraid of the material. The material knows when you are frightened and will not help.‚Äù¬Ý

One from Boccaccio: ‚ÄúWho in our day can penetrate the hearts of the Ancients? Who can bring to light and life again minds long since removed in death? Who can elicit their meaning? A divine task that‚Äînot human! It is, therefore, my plan of interpretation first to write what I learn from the Ancients, and when they fail me, or I find them inexplicit, to set down my own opinion.‚Äù¬Ý

And one from Bob Johnson to Johnny Cash: ‚ÄúYou need to build a mausoleum in your head with big iron doors so that nobody can get in there except you. You don‚Äôt let me in there, you don‚Äôt let June in there, you don‚Äôt let your manager in there, you don‚Äôt let the record company people in there. You have to decide for yourself what you want to do with your music and not let anyone else tell you.‚Äù

And for the project I am working on now, it‚Äôs a quote from Paul Horgan, a two-time Pulitzer Prize-winning biographer and historian: ‚ÄúHistorical writing which is not literature is subject to oblivion.‚Äù

‚Äî

I have all these reminders because I need them.

On the Daily Stoic podcast, I once asked Pau Gasol‚Äîtwo-time NBA champion, six-time All-Star‚Äîabout the role these kinds of reminders play in sports.¬Ý

‚ÄúAthletes appreciate pointers and directions,‚Äù he said. He mentioned the famous mantra displayed throughout the San Antonio Spurs facilities: Pound the rock. ‚ÄúThat was a big one‚Ä¶Just keep pounding the rock. If you hit it a thousand times or two thousand times, you might not see a crack, but it‚Äôs that next hit, that next pound where the rock will crack. You just got to keep at it, keep at it, keep at it.‚Äù

Reminders matter. They make you better. They keep you centered. If life were easy, if we were perfect, we wouldn‚Äôt need them. But it isn‚Äôt, and we aren‚Äôt. Things get complicated. Things go awry. We get mixed signals and we get overwhelmed and we get knocked off course. In those moments, it helps to have a good reminder to fall back on.

So whatever form these things take for you‚Äîa sign, a notecard, a print, a picture, a quote on the wall, a pine cone on your desk‚Äîsurround yourself with things that mean something to you. Tattoo your walls with precepts. Fill your shelves with totems. Put good advice on and around your desk.

It will make you better.¬Ý

item.title: The Question I Ask Myself At The End of Every Day
item.dc:creator: Ryan Holiday
item.description: Come see me in SF and Portland next month‚ÄîAustralia and the mid-west after that and then the east coast‚Äîas part of the Daily Stoic Live tour. Ask me questions, get your books signed, learn a little‚Äîshould be fun. Grab tickets here! When I started writing, I followed the advice a lot of writers follow: hit a word count. Write a thousand words a day. Two thousand. Whatever the number. Then I came across what, for years, I thought was the single best rule for writers‚Äîthat the way to write a book is by producing ‚Äútwo crappy pages a day.‚Äù Not brilliant pages. Not polished pages. Just two crappy ones. Give yourself permission to be bad. But over time, I’ve come to lower the stakes even further. Because even ‚Äútwo crappy pages a day‚Äù as a metric still creates a kind of perverse incentive. They say that what gets measured, gets managed, right? Metrics are a statement of your values and priorities. The problem with measuring output in pages is that it implies that adding pages is what you should be doing every day. Like that‚Äôs the job and it most certainly isn‚Äôt. Now my writing habit is simpler, easier, and in a way, much better. My rule is just, make a positive contribution every day. The question I ask myself at the end of the day is simply, Did I make a positive contribution to my writing today? That‚Äôs it. Sometimes a ‚Äúpositive contribution‚Äù means writing a bunch of new pages. Sometimes it means editing a chapter. Sometimes it’s adding. Sometimes it’s deleting. Sometimes it means I just have a really good phone call about the book with someone whose opinion matters. Maybe I read something good. Maybe I went for a long walk and thought of something that excited me about it. Maybe I met someone who can help me when it comes out.¬Ý This is something I can do anywhere‚Ä¶it‚Äôs something I can do any day. I can do it when I‚Äôm sick. I can do it when I‚Äôm motivated. I can do it from my office or a hotel room or on the road. It can be a little contribution or a big one. And at some point it hit me‚Äîthis isn‚Äôt a practice for writing. It‚Äôs a practice for life. Every year, as part of the New Year, New You Challenge we do over at Daily Stoic, we do weekly live calls. And year after year, one of the clearest patterns in the questions people ask is the struggle with all-or-nothing thinking. Not just with the challenge itself, but in their day-to-day lives.¬Ý One person talked about abandoning a creative project because their schedule didn‚Äôt allow for the long, uninterrupted sessions they felt was required. Another talked about feeling like they had a terrible reading year because their (arbitrary) goal was to read fifty books, and they only read thirty-three. Several talked about how they don‚Äôt exercise, meditate, or journal at all because if you only have five or ten minutes, what‚Äôs the point?¬Ý It‚Äôs the same binary, all-or-nothing trap, again and again. Either I‚Äôm all in, or I’m out. Either I can commit fully, or I shouldn’t bother at all. Either I did the thing perfectly, or it was a complete failure. In ‚ÄãDiscipline Is Destiny‚Äã, I write about the practice of Kaizen, the Japanese philosophy of continuous improvement. Always finding some way to make a little progress. Focusing on the joys of getting a little bit better, day after day. Because over time, it accumulates and compounds into impressive outcomes.¬Ý Think about it: Most people don‚Äôt even show up. Of the people who do, most don‚Äôt really push themselves. So to show up and be disciplined about daily improvement? You are the rarest of the rare. That‚Äôs the question you want to consider. Not, what does the perfect, optimal, most ideal version of this look like? But, How much progress could I make if I made just a small positive contribution each day over the course of an entire life? In one of his most famous letters to Lucilius, Seneca gives a pretty simple prescription for the good life. ‚ÄúEach day,‚Äù he wrote, ‚Äúacquire something that will fortify you against poverty, against death, indeed against other misfortunes, as well and after you have run over many thoughts, select one to be thoroughly digested that day.‚Äù¬Ý One gain per day. Or not even that‚Äîjust spending some time seriously thinking one thing over per day. That‚Äôs it.¬Ý George Washington‚Äôs favorite saying was ‚Äúmany mickles make a muckle.‚Äù It was an old Scottish proverb that illustrates a truth we all know: things add up. Even little ones. Even at the pace of one per day.¬Ý The Stoics believed it was the little things that added up to wisdom and to virtue. What you read. Who you studied under. What you prioritized. How you treated someone. What your routine was like. The training you underwent. What rules you followed. What habits you cultivated. Day to day, practiced over a lifetime, this is what created greatness. This is what led to a good life. ‚ÄúWell-being is realized by small steps,‚Äù Zeno would say looking back on his life, ‚Äúbut is truly no small thing.‚Äù So as a writer and as a person, I try to focus on just making a small contribution every day. I know that cumulatively this has an enormous impact. It‚Äôs not as glamorous as transformative reinvention or bold, dramatic leaps. But it‚Äôs dependable and it works. It‚Äôs something I control.¬Ý ‚ÄúDo the best you can,‚Äù the emperor says in Marguerite Yourcenar‚Äôs beautiful novel Memoirs of Hadrian. ‚ÄúDo it over again. Then still improve, even if ever so slightly those retouches.‚Äù¬Ý It‚Äôs a beautiful irony: You‚Äôre never content with your progress and yet, you‚Äôre always content . . . because you‚Äôre making progress. You‚Äôre making a positive contribution. Every day.
item.content:encoded:
Come see me in SF and Portland next month‚ÄîAustralia and the mid-west after that and then the east coast‚Äîas part of the Daily Stoic Live tour. Ask me questions, get your books signed, learn a little‚Äîshould be fun. Grab tickets here!

When I started writing, I followed the advice a lot of writers follow: hit a word count. Write a thousand words a day. Two thousand. Whatever the number.

Then I came across what, for years, I thought was the single best rule for writers‚Äîthat the way to write a book is by producing ‚Äútwo crappy pages a day.‚Äù Not brilliant pages. Not polished pages. Just two crappy ones. Give yourself permission to be bad.

But over time, I’ve come to lower the stakes even further.

Because even ‚Äútwo crappy pages a day‚Äù as a metric still creates a kind of perverse incentive. They say that what gets measured, gets managed, right? Metrics are a statement of your values and priorities. The problem with measuring output in pages is that it implies that adding pages is what you should be doing every day. Like that‚Äôs the job and it most certainly isn‚Äôt.

Now my writing habit is simpler, easier, and in a way, much better. My rule is just, make a positive contribution every day. The question I ask myself at the end of the day is simply, Did I make a positive contribution to my writing today?

That‚Äôs it.

Sometimes a ‚Äúpositive contribution‚Äù means writing a bunch of new pages. Sometimes it means editing a chapter. Sometimes it’s adding. Sometimes it’s deleting. Sometimes it means I just have a really good phone call about the book with someone whose opinion matters. Maybe I read something good. Maybe I went for a long walk and thought of something that excited me about it. Maybe I met someone who can help me when it comes out.¬Ý

This is something I can do anywhere‚Ä¶it‚Äôs something I can do any day. I can do it when I‚Äôm sick. I can do it when I‚Äôm motivated. I can do it from my office or a hotel room or on the road. It can be a little contribution or a big one.

And at some point it hit me‚Äîthis isn‚Äôt a practice for writing. It‚Äôs a practice for life.

Every year, as part of the New Year, New You Challenge we do over at Daily Stoic, we do weekly live calls. And year after year, one of the clearest patterns in the questions people ask is the struggle with all-or-nothing thinking. Not just with the challenge itself, but in their day-to-day lives.¬Ý

One person talked about abandoning a creative project because their schedule didn‚Äôt allow for the long, uninterrupted sessions they felt was required. Another talked about feeling like they had a terrible reading year because their (arbitrary) goal was to read fifty books, and they only read thirty-three. Several talked about how they don‚Äôt exercise, meditate, or journal at all because if you only have five or ten minutes, what‚Äôs the point?¬Ý

It‚Äôs the same binary, all-or-nothing trap, again and again. Either I‚Äôm all in, or I’m out. Either I can commit fully, or I shouldn’t bother at all. Either I did the thing perfectly, or it was a complete failure.

In ‚ÄãDiscipline Is Destiny‚Äã, I write about the practice of Kaizen, the Japanese philosophy of continuous improvement. Always finding some way to make a little progress. Focusing on the joys of getting a little bit better, day after day.

Because over time, it accumulates and compounds into impressive outcomes.¬Ý

Think about it: Most people don‚Äôt even show up. Of the people who do, most don‚Äôt really push themselves. So to show up and be disciplined about daily improvement? You are the rarest of the rare.

That‚Äôs the question you want to consider. Not, what does the perfect, optimal, most ideal version of this look like? But, How much progress could I make if I made just a small positive contribution each day over the course of an entire life?

In one of his most famous letters to Lucilius, Seneca gives a pretty simple prescription for the good life. ‚ÄúEach day,‚Äù he wrote, ‚Äúacquire something that will fortify you against poverty, against death, indeed against other misfortunes, as well and after you have run over many thoughts, select one to be thoroughly digested that day.‚Äù¬Ý

One gain per day. Or not even that‚Äîjust spending some time seriously thinking one thing over per day. That‚Äôs it.¬Ý

George Washington‚Äôs favorite saying was ‚Äúmany mickles make a muckle.‚Äù It was an old Scottish proverb that illustrates a truth we all know: things add up. Even little ones. Even at the pace of one per day.¬Ý

The Stoics believed it was the little things that added up to wisdom and to virtue. What you read. Who you studied under. What you prioritized. How you treated someone. What your routine was like. The training you underwent. What rules you followed. What habits you cultivated. Day to day, practiced over a lifetime, this is what created greatness. This is what led to a good life.

‚ÄúWell-being is realized by small steps,‚Äù Zeno would say looking back on his life, ‚Äúbut is truly no small thing.‚Äù

So as a writer and as a person, I try to focus on just making a small contribution every day. I know that cumulatively this has an enormous impact. It‚Äôs not as glamorous as transformative reinvention or bold, dramatic leaps. But it‚Äôs dependable and it works. It‚Äôs something I control.¬Ý

‚ÄúDo the best you can,‚Äù the emperor says in Marguerite Yourcenar‚Äôs beautiful novel Memoirs of Hadrian. ‚ÄúDo it over again. Then still improve, even if ever so slightly those retouches.‚Äù¬Ý

It‚Äôs a beautiful irony: You‚Äôre never content with your progress and yet, you‚Äôre always content . . . because you‚Äôre making progress.

You‚Äôre making a positive contribution.

Every day.

item.title: This Is The Most Important Skill You Can Have In Life
item.dc:creator: Ryan Holiday
item.description: I‚Äôm going on tour next month (Portland and SF) and then a bunch of other places around the world in the early fall. Come see me talking Stoicism‚Äîgrab tickets here.¬Ý I hated writing essays in high school, but they changed my life. Not because of the subject matter or anything. With one exception, I can‚Äôt remember what any of them were about. The one I remember is an 11th-grade English assignment. My teacher had us write an essay on this prompt: ‚ÄúAnalyze how and why The Great Gatsby is an exploration of the American Dream as it exists in a corrupt period.‚Äù¬Ý The day after I handed in my essay, Mrs. Kars printed it out and we spent the entire period reviewing it with the class. It was the first time anything I had ever written had been recognized. Now that I think about it, it may have been one of the first times in my life that I had ever felt like I might be anything but average.¬Ý I would later ask Mrs. Kars ‚Äãfor a letter of recommendation‚Äã to a college I did not get accepted to, but I can still remember a line from it. ‚ÄúI have no doubt,‚Äù she said, ‚Äúthat Ryan will someday be a literary giant.‚Äù I don‚Äôt know about that. But I can say that that essay‚Äîwhich upon re-reading is not that good‚Äîand the many others I had to write did what essays have done for generations of young people: they taught me how to use my brain. In having to write them, I learned how to think, I learned to think hard about something and then most importantly how to articulate what I thought about it. This is slow, tedious, difficult work. It takes discipline and patience. The hours and hours of sitting with frustration and confusion. It takes trial and error.¬Ý In Wisdom Takes Work, I tell a story about Eisenhower when he was a promising young general. Just days after Pearl Harbor in 1941, he was called in to see George Marshall, the chief of staff of the US Army. Japan was going to seize the Philippines and dozens of other islands in the Pacific, Marshall explained. America faced a war in two theaters with supply lines stretching thousands of miles. ‚ÄúWhat should be our general line of action?‚Äù Marshall asked Eisenhower. A certain type of officer would have started thinking out loud, riffed, brainstormed. Not Eisenhower. His career and potentially millions of lives hung in the balance.¬Ý ‚ÄúGive me a few hours,‚Äù he said. At a spare desk in the War Plans Division, Eisenhower requisitioned paper, a pen, and a typewriter and got to work. What did Marshall want to accomplish? What was possible? What was of the highest priority? What risks were acceptable?¬Ý After a period of reflection, he wrote his thoughts out. What Marshall needed was ‚Äúshort, emphatic‚Ä¶reasoning,‚Äù not ‚Äúoratory, plausible argument, or glittering generality.‚Äù The writing exercise helped him synthesize ideas from conversations he‚Äôd had with his mentor General Fox Conner, from books he‚Äôd read, from courses at the Army War College, and from his three decades in uniform. As dusk fell, Eisenhower handed Marshall a three-hundred-word briefing on yellow lined paper titled ‚ÄúAssistance to the Far East/Steps to Be Taken.‚Äù Almost certainly Marshall had already considered most of what Eisenhower had written. The assignment was, in a way, a test. What kind of a thinker was this young officer? How did he approach problems? How good was he at responding under pressure? Could he see the big picture? Could he effectively communicate what he knew and what he wanted to do? ‚ÄúI agree with you,‚Äù Marshall eventually replied about Eisenhower‚Äôs plan, and then told him to execute it. Thus began one of the most effective partnerships of the war, propelling Eisenhower to the presidency.¬Ý Successful campaigns and careers‚Äîwhether they involve leading people into battle or saving their souls or selling them things‚Äîdepend on this kind of thinking process. This is what worries me about what AI is doing to writing‚Äîand the school essay especially.¬Ý As I write this line, not only does software make suggestions on spelling and help me eliminate errors, it suggests how I might finish sentences or word them better. If I want, I could simply click over to other software and ask it to write the draft for me. But these fast, easy ways to produce what resembles a finished piece of writing would defeat the purpose. Which is to engage and struggle with the material for an extended period of time. To take my time. To go over things again and again. To be immersed. To be focused, patient, and disciplined. To come to understand things deeply.¬Ý A couple of years ago, I asked Robert Greene what ‚Äãhe thought about AI. ‚ÄúI think back to when I was 19-years-old and in college,‚Äù Robert said. It was a class where they were¬Ý to read and translate classical Greek texts ‚ÄúThey gave us a passage of Thucydides, the hardest writer of all to read in ancient Greek,‚Äù he explained. ‚ÄúI had this one paragraph I must have spent ten hours trying to translate‚Ä¶That had an incredible impact on me. It developed character, patience, and discipline that helps me even to this day. What if I had ChatGPT, and I put the passage in there, and it gave me the translation right away? The whole thinking process would have been annihilated right there.‚Äù For an entire generation of young people, the whole thinking process is being annihilated. How will they figure out what they think? How will they develop critical thinking skills? How will they develop focus, patience, discipline? How will they come to understand things deeply? We think as we write. Indeed, we cannot finish a sentence until we have carried the thought all the way through. We ponder opposing ideas as we pause between keystrokes, the pen becomes our third eye. On the page we see the pattern. Transcribing the [...]
item.content:encoded:
I‚Äôm going on tour next month (Portland and SF) and then a bunch of other places around the world in the early fall. Come see me talking Stoicism‚Äîgrab tickets here.¬Ý

I hated writing essays in high school, but they changed my life.

Not because of the subject matter or anything. With one exception, I can‚Äôt remember what any of them were about.

The one I remember is an 11th-grade English assignment. My teacher had us write an essay on this prompt: ‚ÄúAnalyze how and why The Great Gatsby is an exploration of the American Dream as it exists in a corrupt period.‚Äù¬Ý

The day after I handed in my essay, Mrs. Kars printed it out and we spent the entire period reviewing it with the class.

It was the first time anything I had ever written had been recognized. Now that I think about it, it may have been one of the first times in my life that I had ever felt like I might be anything but average.¬Ý

I would later ask Mrs. Kars ‚Äãfor a letter of recommendation‚Äã to a college I did not get accepted to, but I can still remember a line from it. ‚ÄúI have no doubt,‚Äù she said, ‚Äúthat Ryan will someday be a literary giant.‚Äù

I don‚Äôt know about that. But I can say that that essay‚Äîwhich upon re-reading is not that good‚Äîand the many others I had to write did what essays have done for generations of young people: they taught me how to use my brain. In having to write them, I learned how to think, I learned to think hard about something and then most importantly how to articulate what I thought about it.

This is slow, tedious, difficult work. It takes discipline and patience. The hours and hours of sitting with frustration and confusion. It takes trial and error.¬Ý

In Wisdom Takes Work, I tell a story about Eisenhower when he was a promising young general. Just days after Pearl Harbor in 1941, he was called in to see George Marshall, the chief of staff of the US Army. Japan was going to seize the Philippines and dozens of other islands in the Pacific, Marshall explained. America faced a war in two theaters with supply lines stretching thousands of miles. ‚ÄúWhat should be our general line of action?‚Äù Marshall asked Eisenhower.

A certain type of officer would have started thinking out loud, riffed, brainstormed. Not Eisenhower. His career and potentially millions of lives hung in the balance.¬Ý

‚ÄúGive me a few hours,‚Äù he said. At a spare desk in the War Plans Division, Eisenhower requisitioned paper, a pen, and a typewriter and got to work. What did Marshall want to accomplish? What was possible? What was of the highest priority? What risks were acceptable?¬Ý

After a period of reflection, he wrote his thoughts out. What Marshall needed was ‚Äúshort, emphatic‚Ä¶reasoning,‚Äù not ‚Äúoratory, plausible argument, or glittering generality.‚Äù The writing exercise helped him synthesize ideas from conversations he‚Äôd had with his mentor General Fox Conner, from books he‚Äôd read, from courses at the Army War College, and from his three decades in uniform. As dusk fell, Eisenhower handed Marshall a three-hundred-word briefing on yellow lined paper titled ‚ÄúAssistance to the Far East/Steps to Be Taken.‚Äù

Almost certainly Marshall had already considered most of what Eisenhower had written. The assignment was, in a way, a test. What kind of a thinker was this young officer? How did he approach problems? How good was he at responding under pressure? Could he see the big picture? Could he effectively communicate what he knew and what he wanted to do?

‚ÄúI agree with you,‚Äù Marshall eventually replied about Eisenhower‚Äôs plan, and then told him to execute it. Thus began one of the most effective partnerships of the war, propelling Eisenhower to the presidency.¬Ý

Successful campaigns and careers‚Äîwhether they involve leading people into battle or saving their souls or selling them things‚Äîdepend on this kind of thinking process.

This is what worries me about what AI is doing to writing‚Äîand the school essay especially.¬Ý

As I write this line, not only does software make suggestions on spelling and help me eliminate errors, it suggests how I might finish sentences or word them better. If I want, I could simply click over to other software and ask it to write the draft for me. But these fast, easy ways to produce what resembles a finished piece of writing would defeat the purpose. Which is to engage and struggle with the material for an extended period of time. To take my time. To go over things again and again. To be immersed. To be focused, patient, and disciplined. To come to understand things deeply.¬Ý

A couple of years ago, I asked Robert Greene what ‚Äãhe thought about AI. ‚ÄúI think back to when I was 19-years-old and in college,‚Äù Robert said. It was a class where they were¬Ý to read and translate classical Greek texts ‚ÄúThey gave us a passage of Thucydides, the hardest writer of all to read in ancient Greek,‚Äù he explained. ‚ÄúI had this one paragraph I must have spent ten hours trying to translate‚Ä¶That had an incredible impact on me. It developed character, patience, and discipline that helps me even to this day. What if I had ChatGPT, and I put the passage in there, and it gave me the translation right away? The whole thinking process would have been annihilated right there.‚Äù

For an entire generation of young people, the whole thinking process is being annihilated. How will they figure out what they think? How will they develop critical thinking skills? How will they develop focus, patience, discipline? How will they come to understand things deeply?

We think as we write. Indeed, we cannot finish a sentence until we have carried the thought all the way through. We ponder opposing ideas as we pause between keystrokes, the pen becomes our third eye. On the page we see the pattern. Transcribing the passage or a quote, we get to feel real genius and insight pass through our mind and our fingers, processing each word, weighing and understanding the wisdom. We see what we didn‚Äôt see before. And when we take edits and feedback from others, we see even more, because editing is a kind of interrogation, a process by which we are refining and sharpening our thinking, a way to get our story straight.¬Ý

Joan Didion described writing as a ‚Äúhostile act.‚Äù By that she meant that the writer is trying ‚Äúto make somebody see something the way you see it, trying to impose your idea, your picture.‚Äù But Keynes was even closer to the mark when he referred to writing as the ‚Äúassault of thought on the unthinking.‚Äù A battle against our own wild thoughts, against the preconceived assumptions of others, against all the alternative ideas (and tempting facts) out there.¬Ý

The purpose of the school essay‚Äîof any piece of writing at all‚Äîis not the end product on the page. It‚Äôs the person YOU are on the other side of having done it. It‚Äôs the thinking long and hard about something. It‚Äôs the slow, tedious, difficult work of figuring out what you actually work. And the equally hard work of finding the words for what you think.¬Ý

AI can you give you an essay, an article, a book, or a briefing.

What it can‚Äôt give you is the person you can only become by doing the writing yourself.

item.title: 20 Years Ago, I Spent $8 on This. My Life Was Never The Same.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Holiday
item.description: Quick note: I‚Äôm hitting the road for a Stoicism speaking tour throughout the rest of the year. So far, dates are set for Portland, SF, Chicago, Minneapolis, Detroit, Boston, Philadelphia, Washington, Australia, and New Zealand, with more to come. Get tickets (and updates when new dates are added) here! Sitting on my desk as I write this is a book I paid $8.25 for nearly twenty years ago. The cover is taped back on. Nearly every page is marked or folded. They have yellowed with age, in some cases becoming almost translucent. Nearly every passage has something noted or underlined. There are spills and stains everywhere.¬Ý I bought it when I was 19 years old. I had been invited to a small, private summit of college journalists that Dr. Drew, then the host of Loveline, was hosting. After it ended, he was standing in the corner and I cautiously made my way over to nervously ask if he had any book recommendations. He said he’d been studying a Stoic philosopher named Epictetus and that I should check it out. I went back to my hotel room, and I can‚Äôt remember why, but I didn‚Äôt order the Epictetus book. Maybe I did a quick search about Stoic philosophy and learned that Marcus Aurelius was also a Stoic, and since I knew him from Gladiator, I decided to start there? I don‚Äôt know. But I ended up ordering Marcus‚Äô Meditations, along with a couple other books I‚Äôd had my eye on to qualify for free shipping (Amazon Prime didn‚Äôt exist back then). I seem to remember my copy arriving right away after I purchased it, but recently searching my email for the order number, I found an angry customer service ticket, where teenage-me is angrily complaining about a few days‚Äô shipping delay. How badly I needed the words I would find in Book 6 of Meditations! ‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt have to turn this into something,‚Äù Marcus writes. ‚ÄúIt doesn’t have to upset you.‚Äù I had no idea that the money I spent on that book‚Äîand the couple days of waiting‚Äîwould become the single best time and money I ever spent. I had no idea that it would change the course of my life. And I had no idea that I was just another link in a multi-century chain of people discovering that the right book at the right time is a powerful thing. In fact, it can change the whole course of your life. Marcus Aurelius himself would probably understand my feelings here, for he himself notes in the opening pages his gratitude to his philosophy teacher, Rusticus, ‚Äúfor introducing me to Epictetus’s lectures‚Äîand loaning me his own copy.‚Äù The life of a future king was changed by the wisdom of a Greek slave who had triumphed over torture and exile, whose lectures were fortuitously recorded by a student in the early second century A.D. (and, just as unpredictably, survive to be read by us today). Epictetus himself found freedom from slavery, long before he was legally free. How? In the writings of the Stoics, in the words of Musonius Rufus. He read his way to freedom, literally and figuratively, as Frederick Douglass would do in America two thousand years later.¬Ý The late basketball coach George Raveling tells the story in his book What You‚Äôre Made For about his grandmother, who raised him. One night, as they were cooking dinner, she told him that back in the days of slavery, plantation owners would hide their money in the books on the shelves of their libraries. ‚ÄúWhy did the slave masters hide their money in books, George?‚Äù she asked him. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know Grandma,‚Äù George replied, ‚Äúwhy did they do that?‚Äù ‚ÄúBecause they knew the slaves couldn‚Äôt read,‚Äù she said, ‚Äúso they would never take the books down.‚Äù It‚Äôs a dark subject to bring up with a young boy. But George‚Äîwho would go on to become the first African American basketball coach in what‚Äôs now the Pac-12, win 2 Olympic medals, and earn a Hall of Fame induction‚Äîsaid it was this early lesson that began his lifelong ‚Äúlove affair with books.‚Äù To me, the moral of that story is not just that there is power in the written word (that‚Äôs why they made it illegal to teach slaves to read), but also that what‚Äôs inside them is very valuable. And the truth is that books still have money between the pages, though not because someone put it there in order to keep it from you.¬Ý Think about how many people want to get better‚Äîat something, anything, everything. Look at how many people are desperate to be successful, or to extricate themselves from this cycle of mediocrity that has trapped so many of our generation. These people look everywhere for the solution to their problems. They seek out secret formulas, shortcuts, gurus. They will turn their entire world upside down before they stop and look at the one place where you can always be sure to find answers‚Äîthe book shelf.¬Ý Warren Buffett is one of the richest men in the world, today worth $140 billion. Do you know what he traces his fortune back to? His single best investment decision? A book! The Intelligent Investor by Benjamin Graham, which he first read at the age of 19. We don‚Äôt know exactly what he paid for it, but in the late 1940s, a hardcover typically went for $1.30. ‚ÄúOf all the investments I ever made,‚Äù he said, ‚Äú[it] was the best.‚Äù In the late 1940s, books would have cost perhaps a dollar but even if Buffett had paid millions for it, it‚Äôd have still been a pretty good ROI. I myself wouldn‚Äôt be writing this to you today if I hadn‚Äôt spent that $8.25 back in 2006. That book didn‚Äôt just teach me about life. It taught me how to write. It schooled me in the art of working with and managing people. It gave me the subject that I‚Äôve now spent nearly two decades [...]
item.content:encoded:
Quick note: I‚Äôm hitting the road for a Stoicism speaking tour throughout the rest of the year. So far, dates are set for Portland, SF, Chicago, Minneapolis, Detroit, Boston, Philadelphia, Washington, Australia, and New Zealand, with more to come. Get tickets (and updates when new dates are added) here!

Sitting on my desk as I write this is a book I paid $8.25 for nearly twenty years ago.

The cover is taped back on. Nearly every page is marked or folded. They have yellowed with age, in some cases becoming almost translucent. Nearly every passage has something noted or underlined. There are spills and stains everywhere.¬Ý

I bought it when I was 19 years old. I had been invited to a small, private summit of college journalists that Dr. Drew, then the host of Loveline, was hosting. After it ended, he was standing in the corner and I cautiously made my way over to nervously ask if he had any book recommendations. He said he’d been studying a Stoic philosopher named Epictetus and that I should check it out.

I went back to my hotel room, and I can‚Äôt remember why, but I didn‚Äôt order the Epictetus book. Maybe I did a quick search about Stoic philosophy and learned that Marcus Aurelius was also a Stoic, and since I knew him from Gladiator, I decided to start there? I don‚Äôt know. But I ended up ordering Marcus‚Äô Meditations, along with a couple other books I‚Äôd had my eye on to qualify for free shipping (Amazon Prime didn‚Äôt exist back then).

I seem to remember my copy arriving right away after I purchased it, but recently searching my email for the order number, I found an angry customer service ticket, where teenage-me is angrily complaining about a few days‚Äô shipping delay.

How badly I needed the words I would find in Book 6 of Meditations! ‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt have to turn this into something,‚Äù Marcus writes. ‚ÄúIt doesn’t have to upset you.‚Äù

I had no idea that the money I spent on that book‚Äîand the couple days of waiting‚Äîwould become the single best time and money I ever spent. I had no idea that it would change the course of my life. And I had no idea that I was just another link in a multi-century chain of people discovering that the right book at the right time is a powerful thing.

In fact, it can change the whole course of your life.

Marcus Aurelius himself would probably understand my feelings here, for he himself notes in the opening pages his gratitude to his philosophy teacher, Rusticus, ‚Äúfor introducing me to Epictetus’s lectures‚Äîand loaning me his own copy.‚Äù The life of a future king was changed by the wisdom of a Greek slave who had triumphed over torture and exile, whose lectures were fortuitously recorded by a student in the early second century A.D. (and, just as unpredictably, survive to be read by us today).

Epictetus himself found freedom from slavery, long before he was legally free. How? In the writings of the Stoics, in the words of Musonius Rufus. He read his way to freedom, literally and figuratively, as Frederick Douglass would do in America two thousand years later.¬Ý

The late basketball coach George Raveling tells the story in his book What You‚Äôre Made For about his grandmother, who raised him. One night, as they were cooking dinner, she told him that back in the days of slavery, plantation owners would hide their money in the books on the shelves of their libraries.

‚ÄúWhy did the slave masters hide their money in books, George?‚Äù she asked him.

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know Grandma,‚Äù George replied, ‚Äúwhy did they do that?‚Äù

‚ÄúBecause they knew the slaves couldn‚Äôt read,‚Äù she said, ‚Äúso they would never take the books down.‚Äù

It‚Äôs a dark subject to bring up with a young boy. But George‚Äîwho would go on to become the first African American basketball coach in what‚Äôs now the Pac-12, win 2 Olympic medals, and earn a Hall of Fame induction‚Äîsaid it was this early lesson that began his lifelong ‚Äúlove affair with books.‚Äù

To me, the moral of that story is not just that there is power in the written word (that‚Äôs why they made it illegal to teach slaves to read), but also that what‚Äôs inside them is very valuable. And the truth is that books still have money between the pages, though not because someone put it there in order to keep it from you.¬Ý

Think about how many people want to get better‚Äîat something, anything, everything. Look at how many people are desperate to be successful, or to extricate themselves from this cycle of mediocrity that has trapped so many of our generation. These people look everywhere for the solution to their problems. They seek out secret formulas, shortcuts, gurus. They will turn their entire world upside down before they stop and look at the one place where you can always be sure to find answers‚Äîthe book shelf.¬Ý

Warren Buffett is one of the richest men in the world, today worth $140 billion. Do you know what he traces his fortune back to? His single best investment decision? A book! The Intelligent Investor by Benjamin Graham, which he first read at the age of 19. We don‚Äôt know exactly what he paid for it, but in the late 1940s, a hardcover typically went for $1.30. ‚ÄúOf all the investments I ever made,‚Äù he said, ‚Äú[it] was the best.‚Äù In the late 1940s, books would have cost perhaps a dollar but even if Buffett had paid millions for it, it‚Äôd have still been a pretty good ROI.

I myself wouldn‚Äôt be writing this to you today if I hadn‚Äôt spent that $8.25 back in 2006. That book didn‚Äôt just teach me about life. It taught me how to write. It schooled me in the art of working with and managing people. It gave me the subject that I‚Äôve now spent nearly two decades writing my own books about‚Äîbooks that have somehow sold millions of copies and helped bring this two-thousand-year-old philosophy back into the world.

Again, not a bad ROI!

And of course, the real investment wasn‚Äôt the money I spent on the book. It‚Äôs the time I‚Äôve spent and continue to spend on it. The time spent reading it. The time spent rereading it. The time spent reading Gregory Hays‚Äôs peerless introductory remarks that preface his translation. The time spent reading other editions (e.g. Robin Waterfield‚Äôs fully annotated edition). The time spent reading other books (e.g. Pierre Hadot‚Äôs Inner Citadel: The Meditations of Marcus Aurelius) and essays (e.g. Matthew Arnold‚Äôs Marcus Aurelius) about the book. The time spent reading biographies about Marcus (e.g. Donald Robertson‚Äôs Marcus Aurelius: The Stoic Emperor). The time spent tracking down and interviewing people like Hays, Waterfield, and Robertson. The time spent traveling to Rome to immerse myself in the world he lived in (see the picture at the top of this article).¬Ý

In one of the first passages in Meditations, Marcus said one of the things he learned from his philosophy teacher Rusticus was ‚Äúto read attentively‚Äînot to be satisfied with ‚Äòjust getting the gist of it.‚Äô‚Äù If I‚Äôd stopped at ‚Äújust getting the gist‚Äù of Meditations, my entire life would have turned out differently.

Buffett didn‚Äôt just stop at ‚Äúthe gist‚Äù either. He didn‚Äôt just read The Intelligent Investor. He read it and reread it and reread it. And a few years later, he decided to apply to Columbia Business School where Graham was a teacher. He went ‚Äústraight to the seat of intelligence,‚Äù as Marcus wrote of his own development as a leader, and struck up a friendship with Graham, who later hired Buffett.

It‚Äôs not enough to read‚Äîyou have to go down rabbit holes, look up words you don‚Äôt know, reach out to experts, share interesting ideas with others, earmark pages, and make notes in the margins.¬Ý

FYI: This is the idea behind the Daily Stoic How to Read Meditations Guide we put together‚Äîa digital guide that distills all of my reading, rereading, and rabbit-holing of Meditations into a kind of roadmap designed to help you get out of the book in a few weeks what took me twenty years. As part of Meditations Month, as we refer to April over at Daily Stoic, get the guide before April 26th‚ÄîMarcus‚Äôs birthday‚Äîto join us in the live Q&A I‚Äôm hosting on the 27th!

Some people might recoil at all this talk categorizing books as investments, as things that owe us a return of some kind. But that‚Äôs exactly what books are. That‚Äôs exactly what makes them unique. In Book 1 of Meditations, Marcus himself writes that it was one of the great lessons he learned from his great-grandfather‚Äîto invest in learning ‚Äúand to accept the resulting costs as money well-spent.‚Äù

It‚Äôs why one of my reading rules is, if you see a book you want, just buy it. Don‚Äôt worry about the price. Reading is not a luxury. It‚Äôs not something you splurge on. It‚Äôs a necessity. Even if all you get is one life-changing idea from a book, that‚Äôs still a pretty good investment.¬Ý

Have I applied this rule and bought books that turned out to be duds? Hundreds of times. Maybe thousands. But that’s how any investment strategy works. The winners pay for the losers, and then some.¬Ý

So never forget that it‚Äôs in your self-interest to read. There‚Äôs incredible power and money hidden in books.

But only if you spend the time.¬Ý

Only if you go way beyond the ‚Äúgist.‚Äù

P.S. As I mentioned briefly above, April has been Meditations Month over at Daily Stoic, where I‚Äôm rereading Meditations alongside our Daily Stoic Community as part of our How to Read Meditations Digital Guide.¬Ý

In addition to the 11 in-depth modules of the guide, we‚Äôre having discussions on our private platform and I‚Äôm hosting a live Q&A on April 27th, where I’ll take your questions.¬Ý

Just get the guide before April 26th (Marcus Aurelius‚Äô birthday) to receive your invite to the Q&A.

This month only, you can also purchase the leatherbound edition of ‚ÄãMeditations to get the digital guide and access to the Meditations Month exclusives FREE. Just head to dailystoic.com/meditations.¬Ý

I hope to see you on the 27th!

item.title: This Is Something You Should Always Carry With You
item.dc:creator: Ryan Holiday
item.description: I‚Äôm going to be on a Stoicism speaking tour this summer and fall‚ÄîPortland, SF, Chicago, Minneapolis, Detroit plus a bunch of dates in Australia and New Zealand. Come see me! I‚Äôve brought it to the Grammys. I‚Äôve brought it to NFL games‚Ä¶and kids BJJ practices.¬Ý I‚Äôve brought it into the green room backstage before talks. I‚Äôve brought it to restaurants and bars. I‚Äôve carried it into museums and meetings and to Disneyland.¬Ý I’ve brought it to use before surgery, on planes, beaches, in cars, lines, waiting rooms, helicopters, at the White House, at the DMV, to zoos and parks, car dealerships and shopping malls, while waiting for a movie to start, and on and on. I bring it everywhere. Phone, wallet, keys‚Äîas Adam Sandler says‚Äîand a book.¬Ý I am always carrying one and so should you.¬Ý People often assume something about me: that I‚Äôm a speed reader. It‚Äôs the most common email I get. They see all the books I recommend every month in my reading newsletter and assume I must have some secret. They want to know my trick for reading so fast.¬Ý The truth is, even though I read hundreds of books each year, I actually read at a pretty normal pace. In fact, I deliberately read slowly. But what I also do is read all the time. I always carry a book with me. Every time I get a second, I crack it open. I don‚Äôt install games on my phone‚Äîthat‚Äôs time for reading. When I‚Äôm eating, on a plane, in a waiting room, or sitting in traffic in an Uber‚ÄîI read.¬Ý It‚Äôs an old habit actually. For centuries, busy people have made sure they always had a book within reach. In her book The Bully Pulpit: Theodore Roosevelt, William Howard Taft, and the Golden Age of Journalism, Doris Kearns Goodwin writes about how Roosevelt prioritized his reading time, ‚Äúsnatching moments while waiting for lunch or his next appointment.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe always carried a book with him to the Executive Office,‚Äù Taft recalled, ‚Äúand although there were but few intervals during the business hours, he made the most of them in his reading.‚Äù Before the Vietnam War, James Stockdale was given a copy of Epictetus by one of his professors at Stanford. Soon after, in a three-year span, Stockdale spent three seven-month missions in the waters off Vietnam. He was flying in combat near daily, ‚Äúbut on my bedside table, no matter what carrier I was aboard,‚Äù Stockdale said, ‚Äúwere my Epictetus books…I didn‚Äôt have time to be a bookworm, but I spent several hours each week buried in them.‚Äù Those weren‚Äôt consecutive hours, one must imagine, but little chunks here or there, stolen away‚Äîturning dead time into alive time, as Robert Greene famously said. Look, I get it. You have kids. You have a job‚Äîmaybe two. You have these things you are trying to accomplish. You have to get to the gym. You have all these projects around the house.¬Ý With all this, you say, I just don‚Äôt have time to read. And maybe it‚Äôs true that you don‚Äôt have time to be buried in a book several hours a day. Who does? But you can snatch a few pages here, a few pages there‚Äîon your commute, while the coffee brews, between meetings, over lunch, every time you‚Äôd otherwise reach for your phone. Use every pocket of time you get! And if you never crack it open‚Äîwell, books make great accessories. My wife has a tote bag with a cartoon of a guy packing a book before leaving the house, captioned: ‚ÄúI better bring my book just in case I want to spend all day carrying my book.‚Äù I have plenty of those days. I lug around a four-pound, 900-page biography and the book never leaves my bag. Or I end up taking it for a walk, tucked under my arm, and never actually get time to open it. But I‚Äôve never regretted bringing it. I‚Äôve only ever regretted leaving it behind. It‚Äôs like a little Flat Stanley that I show the sights too‚Ä¶or like a handweight to add some resistance to my everyday activity.¬Ý Does it mean they get dirty and beaten up? That the corners fray and the covers get a little battered? Yes, but that‚Äôs what books are for. Books are not precious things. They are durable, well-designed pieces of technology. As an author, I love it when people hand me a book to sign that has had real miles put on it. When people hand me a pristine copy and tell me it‚Äôs their favorite, I assume they are just flattering me. A well-worn book is a well-loved book. It‚Äôs obvious what my favorite books are‚Ä¶because they‚Äôre falling apart (here‚Äôs my copy of Meditations for instance) or filled with food stains (lol, here are some pictures). Is your phone also a book? Sure. But the point is, we all want to spend less time on the phone and in front of screens. There is something about a physical book that your phone will never replicate‚Äîthe weight of it, the feel of the pages, the fact that it does exactly one thing. It doesn‚Äôt buzz with notifications. It doesn‚Äôt tempt you to swipe over to social media the moment your attention wavers. It doesn‚Äôt have an algorithm deciding what you see next. And isn‚Äôt that the irony? We all say we don‚Äôt have time to read‚Ä¶but the screen time app on our phone sure proves otherwise.¬Ý There‚Äôs something else too. Reading a book on a phone doesn‚Äôt look like you‚Äôre reading a book. It just looks like you‚Äôre on your phone. Some of my favorite random encounters have started with someone asking what I‚Äôm reading, or me asking them. I‚Äôve discovered so many great books that way. I‚Äôve gotten to recommend ones I love. I just had a nice conversation at my son‚Äôs lacrosse practice last week. A guy was reading The Count of Monte Cristo and I turned him onto a book [...]
item.content:encoded:
I‚Äôm going to be on a Stoicism speaking tour this summer and fall‚ÄîPortland, SF, Chicago, Minneapolis, Detroit plus a bunch of dates in Australia and New Zealand. Come see me!

I‚Äôve brought it to the Grammys.

I‚Äôve brought it to NFL games‚Ä¶and kids BJJ practices.¬Ý

I‚Äôve brought it into the green room backstage before talks.

I‚Äôve brought it to restaurants and bars.

I‚Äôve carried it into museums and meetings and to Disneyland.¬Ý

I’ve brought it to use before surgery, on planes, beaches, in cars, lines, waiting rooms, helicopters, at the White House, at the DMV, to zoos and parks, car dealerships and shopping malls, while waiting for a movie to start, and on and on.

I bring it everywhere. Phone, wallet, keys‚Äîas Adam Sandler says‚Äîand a book.¬Ý

I am always carrying one and so should you.¬Ý

People often assume something about me: that I‚Äôm a speed reader. It‚Äôs the most common email I get. They see all the books I recommend every month in my reading newsletter and assume I must have some secret. They want to know my trick for reading so fast.¬Ý

The truth is, even though I read hundreds of books each year, I actually read at a pretty normal pace. In fact, I deliberately read slowly. But what I also do is read all the time. I always carry a book with me. Every time I get a second, I crack it open. I don‚Äôt install games on my phone‚Äîthat‚Äôs time for reading. When I‚Äôm eating, on a plane, in a waiting room, or sitting in traffic in an Uber‚ÄîI read.¬Ý

It‚Äôs an old habit actually. For centuries, busy people have made sure they always had a book within reach.

In her book The Bully Pulpit: Theodore Roosevelt, William Howard Taft, and the Golden Age of Journalism, Doris Kearns Goodwin writes about how Roosevelt prioritized his reading time, ‚Äúsnatching moments while waiting for lunch or his next appointment.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe always carried a book with him to the Executive Office,‚Äù Taft recalled, ‚Äúand although there were but few intervals during the business hours, he made the most of them in his reading.‚Äù

Before the Vietnam War, James Stockdale was given a copy of Epictetus by one of his professors at Stanford. Soon after, in a three-year span, Stockdale spent three seven-month missions in the waters off Vietnam. He was flying in combat near daily, ‚Äúbut on my bedside table, no matter what carrier I was aboard,‚Äù Stockdale said, ‚Äúwere my Epictetus books…I didn‚Äôt have time to be a bookworm, but I spent several hours each week buried in them.‚Äù

Those weren‚Äôt consecutive hours, one must imagine, but little chunks here or there, stolen away‚Äîturning dead time into alive time, as Robert Greene famously said.

Look, I get it. You have kids. You have a job‚Äîmaybe two. You have these things you are trying to accomplish. You have to get to the gym. You have all these projects around the house.¬Ý

With all this, you say, I just don‚Äôt have time to read. And maybe it‚Äôs true that you don‚Äôt have time to be buried in a book several hours a day. Who does? But you can snatch a few pages here, a few pages there‚Äîon your commute, while the coffee brews, between meetings, over lunch, every time you‚Äôd otherwise reach for your phone. Use every pocket of time you get!

And if you never crack it open‚Äîwell, books make great accessories. My wife has a tote bag with a cartoon of a guy packing a book before leaving the house, captioned: ‚ÄúI better bring my book just in case I want to spend all day carrying my book.‚Äù I have plenty of those days. I lug around a four-pound, 900-page biography and the book never leaves my bag. Or I end up taking it for a walk, tucked under my arm, and never actually get time to open it. But I‚Äôve never regretted bringing it. I‚Äôve only ever regretted leaving it behind. It‚Äôs like a little Flat Stanley that I show the sights too‚Ä¶or like a handweight to add some resistance to my everyday activity.¬Ý

Does it mean they get dirty and beaten up? That the corners fray and the covers get a little battered? Yes, but that‚Äôs what books are for. Books are not precious things. They are durable, well-designed pieces of technology. As an author, I love it when people hand me a book to sign that has had real miles put on it. When people hand me a pristine copy and tell me it‚Äôs their favorite, I assume they are just flattering me. A well-worn book is a well-loved book. It‚Äôs obvious what my favorite books are‚Ä¶because they‚Äôre falling apart (here‚Äôs my copy of Meditations for instance) or filled with food stains (lol, here are some pictures).

Is your phone also a book? Sure. But the point is, we all want to spend less time on the phone and in front of screens. There is something about a physical book that your phone will never replicate‚Äîthe weight of it, the feel of the pages, the fact that it does exactly one thing. It doesn‚Äôt buzz with notifications. It doesn‚Äôt tempt you to swipe over to social media the moment your attention wavers. It doesn‚Äôt have an algorithm deciding what you see next. And isn‚Äôt that the irony? We all say we don‚Äôt have time to read‚Ä¶but the screen time app on our phone sure proves otherwise.¬Ý

There‚Äôs something else too. Reading a book on a phone doesn‚Äôt look like you‚Äôre reading a book. It just looks like you‚Äôre on your phone. Some of my favorite random encounters have started with someone asking what I‚Äôm reading, or me asking them. I‚Äôve discovered so many great books that way. I‚Äôve gotten to recommend ones I love. I just had a nice conversation at my son‚Äôs lacrosse practice last week. A guy was reading The Count of Monte Cristo and I turned him onto a book that deserves to be better known, Tom Reiss‚Äô The Black Count, which is about Dumas‚Äô father (I was reading The Best and the Brightest, which I‚Äôve dragged around so much recently‚Äîincluding on a camping trip‚Äîthat the paperback is warped and bent). All of that is lost if you‚Äôre just sitting there staring at a screen.¬Ý

Elsewhere in The Bully Pulpit, Goodwin tells the story of a journalist commissioned to write a profile of Roosevelt. At his office in the New York City police department, in the few moments between one meeting ending and the next beginning, Roosevelt picked up a book on the culture of Sioux Indian tribes. The journalist was amazed. It was only enough time to read a page, maybe two.¬Ý

‚ÄúIt is surprising,‚Äù Roosevelt told the journalist, ‚Äúhow much reading a man can do in time usually wasted.‚Äù

That‚Äôs exactly right.¬Ý

Stop wasting time.¬Ý

Bring a book with you everywhere.¬Ý

item.title: 5 Years of Lessons From Running My Own Bookstore
item.dc:creator: Ryan Holiday
item.description: My wife and I were sitting at a cafe in Bastrop, Texas, looking across Main Street at an empty historic storefront. ‚ÄúYou know what would be amazing there?” she said. ‚ÄúA bookstore.‚Äù We started construction on The Painted Porch the first week of March 2020. Somehow, we didn‚Äôt lose all our money. It didn‚Äôt blow up our marriage. It‚Äôs actually been a great experience and, even more surprising, a pretty good business too. Five years in, I‚Äôve learned a lot‚Äîabout business, about books, about myself. Here are some of those lessons: Crazy can be a competitive advantage. Opening a physical bookstore in 2020 seemed crazy. Not just to me‚Äîeveryone said so. Retail was shifting online, books were becoming digital, the pandemic was raging, bookstores were closing‚Äînot opening. But that‚Äôs exactly why it worked. It was crazy because no one else was doing it. It stands out. It‚Äôs different. Look for disconfirmation. As I was thinking about doing the bookstore, I asked a lot of people why I shouldn‚Äôt do it. Not that I was looking to be talked out of it. I was asking so I could hear the concerns, the objections, the risks I hadn’t considered. Every one of them raised something I hadn‚Äôt thought of and then was then able to address before opening.¬Ý Take some risk off the table. Most big, cool, intimidating things in life comes with a certain amount of risk. But just because you take a big risk doesn‚Äôt mean there aren‚Äôt ways to take risk off the table. A great piece of advice I got from Allison Hill, who owns Vroman‚Äôs and Book Soup in Los Angeles, was to make the bookstore a multipurpose space. The Painted Porch is of course not just a bookstore‚Äîit‚Äôs my office, my employees’ office, the place where we record podcasts and film YouTube videos. So if nobody comes in and buys books, we’re not necessarily losing money. Multi-use allows you to do more than you ordinarily would‚Äîacross the board. Think of it as an experiment. When I was kicking around the idea, Tim Ferriss told me to think of it as an experiment. Try it for two years, he said, and if you hate it at the end or it‚Äôs failing, then walk away. This piece of advice was so freeing. It gave me an out‚Äîwhich allowed me to bravely dive in. Because I wasn’t betting my whole life on something, just a contained time commitment. Thinking of every venture, every project as an experiment is a great way to go through life. It lowers the stakes. It minimizes the downside. It lets you take a shot on something that otherwise might be way too intimidating. Don‚Äôt trust conventional wisdom. One of the things I did while I was kicking around the idea is I looked up how expensive it is to start a bookstore. Search results said it was hundreds and hundreds of thousands of dollars‚Äîway more expensive than I was interested in. But then I wanted to question whether that number was real. So then I went and looked up how expensive it was to start an ecommerce business‚Äîsomething like Daily Stoic. Search results said it was hundreds of thousands of dollars more than I‚Äôd spent to start Daily Stoic. That was really helpful‚Äîto learn, oh, these people don‚Äôt really know what they‚Äôre talking about. Or that there‚Äôs a cheaper way, a different way to do it. You don‚Äôt have to do it the way that everyone else does it. Be okay with mediocrity at first. A problem with having really high standards or when you expect a lot of yourself is that it can be hard to start something new. It‚Äôs hard to be comfortable with something that‚Äôs kind of crappy or mediocre or not all the way there. But there‚Äôs a reason most tech start ups think in terms of a minimum viable product. There‚Äôs a great Hemingway line‚Äîwe actually have a shirt with it, and I have a print of it on my wall‚Äîit‚Äôs one of my all-time favorite quotes: the first draft of everything is shit. I love how The Painted Porch is now, but it took years to get it to where it is. It‚Äôs been a continual process of improvement and growth and making changes. Doing interesting things usually pays off. When I was starting out as a writer, an author gave me a piece of advice I‚Äôve never forgotten: If you want to be a great writer, go live an interesting life. He was right. Great art is fueled by great experiences‚Äîor, if not ‚Äúgreat‚Äù experiences, at least interesting ones. That was in the back of my mind with the bookstore. Even if it failed, I knew the experience of trying to open a small business in rural Texas during a pandemic would be filled with stories. And it has been. I‚Äôve drawn on it constantly‚Äîin my writing, my talks, in conversations with people on the podcast. So when you have the choice between the safe, boring path and the interesting one, take the interesting one. It always pays off. Have a unique proposition. Most bookstores carry thousands of titles. The best one in Austin, BookPeople, stocks over 100,000. We carry about 1,000. It was one of the best decisions we made. We only carry books we love. Not only did this make it cheaper and easier to run the bookstore, it makes us stand out. If people want a specific book, they go to a certain trillion-dollar e-commerce behemoth. If people want to discover new books and have a unique experience, they come to us. We are the only bookstore in the world with our selection. Create spectacles. Before we opened the store, I was in Bucharest, Romania for a talk. My host took me into a local bookstore that had an enormous globe hanging from the ceiling. I watched as customer after customer came in to take pictures beneath it, before checking out with books. [...]
item.content:encoded:
My wife and I were sitting at a cafe in Bastrop, Texas, looking across Main Street at an empty historic storefront.

‚ÄúYou know what would be amazing there?” she said. ‚ÄúA bookstore.‚Äù

We started construction on The Painted Porch the first week of March 2020.

Somehow, we didn‚Äôt lose all our money. It didn‚Äôt blow up our marriage. It‚Äôs actually been a great experience and, even more surprising, a pretty good business too.

Five years in, I‚Äôve learned a lot‚Äîabout business, about books, about myself. Here are some of those lessons:

Crazy can be a competitive advantage. Opening a physical bookstore in 2020 seemed crazy. Not just to me‚Äîeveryone said so. Retail was shifting online, books were becoming digital, the pandemic was raging, bookstores were closing‚Äînot opening. But that‚Äôs exactly why it worked. It was crazy because no one else was doing it. It stands out. It‚Äôs different.

Look for disconfirmation. As I was thinking about doing the bookstore, I asked a lot of people why I shouldn‚Äôt do it. Not that I was looking to be talked out of it. I was asking so I could hear the concerns, the objections, the risks I hadn’t considered. Every one of them raised something I hadn‚Äôt thought of and then was then able to address before opening.¬Ý

Take some risk off the table. Most big, cool, intimidating things in life comes with a certain amount of risk. But just because you take a big risk doesn‚Äôt mean there aren‚Äôt ways to take risk off the table. A great piece of advice I got from Allison Hill, who owns Vroman‚Äôs and Book Soup in Los Angeles, was to make the bookstore a multipurpose space. The Painted Porch is of course not just a bookstore‚Äîit‚Äôs my office, my employees’ office, the place where we record podcasts and film YouTube videos. So if nobody comes in and buys books, we’re not necessarily losing money. Multi-use allows you to do more than you ordinarily would‚Äîacross the board.

Think of it as an experiment. When I was kicking around the idea, Tim Ferriss told me to think of it as an experiment. Try it for two years, he said, and if you hate it at the end or it‚Äôs failing, then walk away. This piece of advice was so freeing. It gave me an out‚Äîwhich allowed me to bravely dive in. Because I wasn’t betting my whole life on something, just a contained time commitment. Thinking of every venture, every project as an experiment is a great way to go through life. It lowers the stakes. It minimizes the downside. It lets you take a shot on something that otherwise might be way too intimidating.

Don‚Äôt trust conventional wisdom. One of the things I did while I was kicking around the idea is I looked up how expensive it is to start a bookstore. Search results said it was hundreds and hundreds of thousands of dollars‚Äîway more expensive than I was interested in. But then I wanted to question whether that number was real. So then I went and looked up how expensive it was to start an ecommerce business‚Äîsomething like Daily Stoic. Search results said it was hundreds of thousands of dollars more than I‚Äôd spent to start Daily Stoic. That was really helpful‚Äîto learn, oh, these people don‚Äôt really know what they‚Äôre talking about. Or that there‚Äôs a cheaper way, a different way to do it. You don‚Äôt have to do it the way that everyone else does it.

Be okay with mediocrity at first. A problem with having really high standards or when you expect a lot of yourself is that it can be hard to start something new. It‚Äôs hard to be comfortable with something that‚Äôs kind of crappy or mediocre or not all the way there. But there‚Äôs a reason most tech start ups think in terms of a minimum viable product. There‚Äôs a great Hemingway line‚Äîwe actually have a shirt with it, and I have a print of it on my wall‚Äîit‚Äôs one of my all-time favorite quotes: the first draft of everything is shit. I love how The Painted Porch is now, but it took years to get it to where it is. It‚Äôs been a continual process of improvement and growth and making changes.

Doing interesting things usually pays off. When I was starting out as a writer, an author gave me a piece of advice I‚Äôve never forgotten: If you want to be a great writer, go live an interesting life. He was right. Great art is fueled by great experiences‚Äîor, if not ‚Äúgreat‚Äù experiences, at least interesting ones. That was in the back of my mind with the bookstore. Even if it failed, I knew the experience of trying to open a small business in rural Texas during a pandemic would be filled with stories. And it has been. I‚Äôve drawn on it constantly‚Äîin my writing, my talks, in conversations with people on the podcast. So when you have the choice between the safe, boring path and the interesting one, take the interesting one. It always pays off.

Have a unique proposition. Most bookstores carry thousands of titles. The best one in Austin, BookPeople, stocks over 100,000. We carry about 1,000. It was one of the best decisions we made. We only carry books we love. Not only did this make it cheaper and easier to run the bookstore, it makes us stand out. If people want a specific book, they go to a certain trillion-dollar e-commerce behemoth. If people want to discover new books and have a unique experience, they come to us. We are the only bookstore in the world with our selection.

Create spectacles. Before we opened the store, I was in Bucharest, Romania for a talk. My host took me into a local bookstore that had an enormous globe hanging from the ceiling. I watched as customer after customer came in to take pictures beneath it, before checking out with books. This inspired our now infamous book tower, which I designed to be built on top of an old, broken fireplace. It‚Äôs 20 feet tall and made of some 2,000 books, 4,000 nails, and 40 gallons of glue. It was not cheap. It was not easy. But it‚Äôs probably one of the single best marketing decisions we made. Invariably, almost every customer that comes in takes a picture of it‚Äîplenty more come in because they heard about it and wanted to see it.

The positive externalities are the best part. I‚Äôve gotten a lot out of the bookstore. I‚Äôve learned a lot‚Ä¶about business, about books, about what I‚Äôm capable of. Sales have been strong. But the most rewarding part has been what it‚Äôs done for other people. Putting books we love out in the world. Creating a gathering place for the people in our community. Building something that makes our small town a little better, a little richer, a little more interesting than it was before.¬Ý

Beware of mission creep. Our original plan was that we‚Äôd have only a couple hundred books, only my absolute favorite books. But I’m always reading and discovering new favorites. So the temptation to add and add and add is always there. In the military, they call this mission creep‚Äîa gradual broadening of objectives as a mission progresses. If you are setting out on a project, it‚Äôs something to be aware of.

For everything you add, take something away. There‚Äôs a great story of Mark Parker who, just after he became CEO of Nike, called Steve Jobs for advice. Is there anything Nike should do differently? Parker asked. ‚ÄúJust one thing,‚Äù Jobs said. ‚ÄúNike makes some of the best products in the world. Products that you lust after. But you also make a lot of crap. Just get rid of the crappy stuff and focus on the good stuff.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe was absolutely right,‚Äù Parker said. ‚ÄúWe had to edit.‚Äù Because we‚Äôve always done it this way, is not a good reason. Or in our case, because we‚Äôve always carried this book, is not a good reason. We have to edit.

Have the discipline to not scale. At least once a week, someone asks if we’re going to open a second location. And at least three struggling bookstores have reached out about us acquiring them. The answer is a polite no. “Do Not Go Past The Mark You Aimed For” is one of the most important laws in The 48 Laws of Power. Know when you‚Äôve won. Know what enough is. Know your limits.

Behind mountains are more mountains. That‚Äôs a Haitian proverb I love. My wife suggested opening the bookstore in the fall of 2019. Then COVID delayed us a year. Then we didn’t feel right opening for another year. Then a freak storm and some political incompetence shut down the power grid‚Äîburst pipes, busted roof. Then a global supply chain crisis made books hard to get. There‚Äôs the day-to-day stuff too: employees get sick, the internet goes out, shipments arrive damaged, a toilet leaks, the door won‚Äôt shut properly all of a sudden. But that‚Äôs how it goes. With most things in life, you don‚Äôt overcome one obstacle, you don‚Äôt get through the first, second, or third year of your business, and then suddenly you’re magically done with obstacles. No, it‚Äôs one damn thing after another. Expect it. Work through it. Keep going.
