Learn from the cats. When we were thinking about opening a bookstore, I bought a course from a bookstore consultant. I talked to friends. I talked to bookstore owners while on a book tour. I got a lot of advice, gathered best practices, and learned what worked for others. And yet, the single most popular thing about The Painted Porch is something that never came up‚Ä¶the cats. In 2021, we took a family road trip to Cerro Gordo, the ghost town Brent Underwood has been restoring‚Äîmy kids are obsessed with his YouTube videos‚Äîand came home with two cats who have lived at the bookstore ever since. They‚Äôre literally the most popular thing about the store. As one Yelp reviewer put it: ‚ÄúNice collection of books, clean, very comfy atmosphere, but I‚Äôm not going to lie to the great people of Bastrop‚Ä¶I come for the cats.‚Äù Lol. So yes, do your research. Yes, learn from others. But keep in mind, some of the best parts of any project are things you can‚Äôt possibly predetermine.

Don‚Äôt overlook simple solutions. There‚Äôs a tendency‚Äîespecially when you care a lot about something‚Äîto overthink it. To assume everything has to be big, polished, expensive, professional. But great ideas can be cheap and easy too. One of my favorite bookstores in the world, Gertrude & Alice in Bondi Beach, puts sticky notes inside their books. Just little handwritten notes from employees about why they liked this or that book. No fancy plaques. No expensive signage. We started doing it at The Painted Porch too. It‚Äôs fun, it‚Äôs human, and customers love it.

Do things only you can do. Something that‚Äôs happened with Daily Stoic over the years is as it has grown, so has the number of copycats. And so we’re constantly asking, what can only we do? With the bookstore, we’re lucky to have authors constantly passing through to record the podcast. While they’re here, they sign books. Sometimes we do live events with them. Those books, those experiences‚Äîyou can’t get them anywhere else. With AI tools making it easier and easier to copy and replicate and reproduce, it‚Äôs more important than ever to find and focus on the things only you can do.

Zoom out. When we were doing a small construction project at the bookstore recently, we moved an old antique bar and found some paint on the wall, covered in plaster. Carefully scraping it away, we found a date: January 16, 1922. What was happening in the world that day? Who were the people who stood there and supervised it being painted? What kind of business was in this space a hundred years ago? How many others have come and gone since? It was a humbling reminder: we’re not the first people to try something in this building, and we won’t be the last. Every project, every place, every person is part of something much bigger‚Äîsomething that started long before us and will continue long after.

If you‚Äôre successful, your people should be successful. Nothing feels better than distributing profits or raises to the team. If you don‚Äôt take pleasure in that, you’re doing it wrong, prioritizing the wrong things.

If you‚Äôve always wanted to do it‚Ä¶do it. This has happened to me more than once. When my wife and I moved to a farm, I couldn’t believe how many people said, ‚ÄúI‚Äôve always wanted to do that.‚Äù Same with opening the bookstore. People hear you have a small-town bookstore and they light up‚Äî‚ÄúI‚Äôve always wanted to do that.‚Äù Casey Neistat has a great line: ‚ÄúThe right time is right now.‚Äù If you‚Äôve always wanted to do something, do it. Stop romanticizing it. Stop overthinking it. Try it. Do it small. Do it your way. But do it.

There are many ways to measure success. One of the first things people want to know is how the bookstore is doing, whether it‚Äôs a success. I like to joke, my wife and I are still together, so yes, that‚Äôs a big win. We survived. We kept ourselves together despite it all.¬Ý¬Ý

The real answer is that early on, we asked ourselves, what does success look like? And we decided that success was going to be: becoming more community minded, becoming more responsible, becoming better organized, having more fun, making a positive contribution.

With any project or endeavor, there are many ways to measure success. Has it made you a better person? Has it made your community better? Did it challenge you in ways you needed to be challenged? What metrics actually matter to you? Remembering why you did something‚Äîand how you defined success at the start‚Äîhelps you calibrate your decisions along the way.¬Ý

It helps you know when you‚Äôve won.
